entries.hook: I've been lifting weights for 14 years.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

I've been lifting weights for 14 years.

But 8 weeks ago, I made a life-changing $21 purchase.

1-pound weight plates.

And this *tiny*  helped me make more progress in 2 months than I had in 2 years.

Here's the quick story (and 1 reflection question that will change your life):

--
So I said I've been lifting weights for 14 years – which is half true.

These 14 years has been riddled with ups & downs.

Seasons of beaming motivation and seasons of burnout.

(Chances are you've felt this too)

And after some reflection, I traced these waves back to 1 feeling:

--

The difference between motivation & burnout?

Clear evidence of progress.

When I saw myself improving, I was motivated to keep going.

If weeks went by without any gains, I would burn out.

And I realized, current gym setups encourage this cycle.

--

Here's why:

At most gyms, the smallest jump you can make is 5 pounds.

So if one of your lifts is 100 pounds, that's a 5% jump!

And if the goal is to make weekly progress, you simply can't sustain 5% jumps every week.

So here's where things go wrong:

--

With this setup, you have to do the same weight for weeks in a row.

During this time, you're getting stronger – but your progress is invisible.

You have no feedback loop – so it can feel like you're spinning your wheels.

But here's where the 1-pound plates changed everything:

--

Over the last 8 weeks, I've made progress *every week*

Because instead of jumping 5% per week, these tiny plates let me jump 1%.

And these weekly, 1% micro-gains have me *addicted* to the process – because I see *actual* improvement every week.

Here's why that's important:

--

If I've learned anything in the last 15 years, it's all good things come from compounding.

And compounding comes from doing:

• The absolute basics
• With monotonous consistency
• For a long period of time

Said another way:

• Simplicity
• Consistency
• Patience

--

And with these pillars in mind, I set goals with 2 steps:

1. Pick an outcome I want to achieve

2. Change the goal from "I want to achieve X" to "I want to enjoy every second on the journey to achieving X."

And here's the key to enjoying every second on the journey:

--

95% of success is learning to cherish the journey on the path of monotonous consistency.

And to fall in love with this path, we need to see progress along the way.

Because more tangible progress we see:

• The more momentum we gain
• The easier it becomes to keep going

Now, these last 8 week gave me 1 big realization.

This idea goes far beyond lifting weights.

In any area you're looking to progress, the key is tiny, continuous, tangible progress over the long term.

Said another way, you need to find your micro plates.

So here's the reflection question for you:

• What are you trying to progress with right now?
• How can you find a way to measure tiny progress consistently?

Whether it's 1% per day, per week, or month, or per year!

Find a way to celebrate your progress.

entries.hook: Organic reach is down 65% on LinkedIn.
entries.postBody:
Organic reach is down 65% on LinkedIn. 

But that doesn't mean you can't grow. 

The LinkedIn algorithm has shifted drastically recently, and genuinely...
I think it's changed more in the last 60 days than in the last six months. 

Here's the state of LinkedIn now 

1. The average post gets 65% less impressions.
2. However, engagement per post is up by 12%. 
3. There are 16% more LI ads than in 2024.
4. Posting 1-2x per day is optimal.
5. Carousels & PDFs are performing 1.9x better the median post.
6. AI comments are not as effective. 
7. Video reach has dropped 200%.

The Science of Commenting 

1. AI-generated comments get 5x less author response and 7x less audience engagement.
2. Longer comments = bigger impact: 10+ words boost reach 2x, 15+ words boost 2.5x.
3. Commenting on 10-20 posts increases profile views by 50% and post reach by 10%.
4. Posts live longer. Go back to your posts and stimulate discussions.

(P.S. LinkedIn are trialling impressions scores on comments!)

The Art Of Post Formatting

These are the top three winners: 

🥇 Carousels: Highly engaging front cover, good hook, and 10+ slides.
🥈 Infographics: Complex sheets drive dwell time and reshares.
🥉 Videos: Think TikTok, with 3-second hook, focused on education or inspiration. 

These can also work: 

- Polls: Learn about your ICP by asking questions that cover common pain points. 
- Text & Photo: Use these for personal stories to share your experiences visually.
- Text Only: Create super pre-validated text posts, humorous stories or 1 liners.

Engagement Ratios

Very High Engagement: Repost
High: Good Comment, Dwell Time, Save
Medium: Emoji
Low: Like  

➡️ How To Increase Post-Performance

1. Pick the right format (carousels & cheat sheets). 
2. Write posts between 800-100 words.
3. Shorter sentences increase readability by 30%.
4. Strong ideas + data visualisation boost performance by 36%.
5. Two-sentence hooks perform 20% better. 
 6. Read the cheat sheet for the further points. 

I am hosting: "How To Sell On LinkedIn" For Free On Thursday 6th March at 5.30pm GMT 

Sign up here: https://lnkd.in/eMYw-JvF 

For Cohort 4 Of The Creator Accelerator

- - - -

If you want a high-res PDF of this sheet:

1. Follow Chris Donnelly.
2. Like the post.
3. Repost to your network.
4. Subscribe to: https://lnkd.in/eXSNaDiu

*This report was prepared from analysing ~10,000 accounts and ~100,000 pieces of content over a comparable period (It will be released quarterly). 

Source of data collection:
1. Anonymised LeverBrands.com post repository.
2. Anonymised SayWhat.ai post repository
3. Anonymised https://lnkd.in/eQiWTE9Z repository.

We pulled ~100,000 posts from saywhat.ai, anonymised the data and compared a 60 day period of the success of post formats. 

In the June version of this report, we will include a page 2 of Source Data and Methodology. 

The data itself is objective, the analysis and insights provided are subjective.

entries.hook: Remember this. The strongest men are often the kindest.
entries.postBody:
Remember this.

The strongest men are often the kindest.

The weakest men are often the loudest.

Don't say what you want to tear down, say what you will build.

Don't say what you're against, say what you're for.

It's always easier to destroy than create.

I'm only interested in the creators.

Rest are just kids throwing tantrums.

♻️ Feel free to share so someone else who needs this reminder can see it.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Parkinson's Law: Work expands to fill the time given for its completion.
entries.postBody:
Parkinson's Law: 
 
Work expands to fill the time given for its completion. 
 
That means, as long as you have time to work, there will be work to do. 
 
You can't ever finish everything. 
 
It's a neverending game of more stuff to do. 
 
It doesn't matter how much you eliminate, simplify, automate, or delegate. 
 
You'll never get to the end of emails, projects, meetings, deliverables, etc. 
 
So don't try. 
 
Instead, choose the 20% of important things that move your business forward and quit doing everything else.  
 
Instead, live your life: 
 
- Take walks 
- Go to the gym 
- Read a good book 
- Do something in your community 
 
Work to live. 
 
Don't live to work.

P.S. I'm working on a guide called LIVING: Thoughts on Living With intention.

A breakdown of how to build Life-Work balance.

If that sounds like something you'd enjoy, then join 85,000+ Solopreneurs on my email list: https://lnkd.in/eXv2Mxfg

I'll send it to you when it's done.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: One of the most legendary marketers of all time:
entries.structural: Question post, How-to / Framework post, Case study / Breakdown
entries.postBody:
One of the most legendary marketers of all time: 

David Ogilvy.

In 1982, David wrote an internal memo to the employees of his advertising agency titled "How to write."

And in just 10 bullets he put together a masterclass in effective writing. 

Here's a breakdown of each one:

The memo starts with a clear why.

"The better you write, the higher you will go in Ogilvy & Mather. People who think well, write well. Good writing is not a natural gift. You have to learn to write well."

Replace "Ogilvy & Mather" with any company and this holds true.

1/ Read the Roman-Raphelson book on writing. Read it 3 times.

Every company on Earth would be a better place if this book was required reading.

If you are still sending emails with Walls of Text, order this.

2/ Write the way you talk. Naturally.

"Finding your writing voice" is a waste of time.

You already have your voice—the one you use every day.

Here's how to start using it in your writing:

• Choose a topic
• Record yourself talking about it

Then, transcribe it and start there.

3/ Use short words, short sentences, and short paragraphs.

This one takes practice.

But the easiest way to find when you're being too wordy? 

Read everything aloud before you publish it. 

When you find yourself getting caught up, it's a sign you need to simplify.

4/ Never use jargon words like "reconceptualizes, demassification, attitudinally, judgementally."

When you see someone using jargon, they're hiding their lack of understanding.

An easy solution: pretend you are writing to an 8th grader.

5/ Never write more than two pages on any subject.

99% of books should be blog posts.

And 99% of blog posts should be tweets.

I would preface this by saying: never *publish* more than two pages on any subject.

If it can't fit in two pages, it should be simpler.

6/ Check your quotations.

This one is simple enough. Misquotes are unforced errors.

7/ Never send a letter or memo on the day you write it. Read it aloud the next morning, then edit it.

This is the number one piece of writing advice I give people.

If you are publishing something important, always, always, give it room to breathe.

And always read it aloud.

8/ If it is something important, get a colleague to improve it.

This pairs nicely with point number 7. If it's something really important, write it, give it a day, edit it, then send it to a colleague.

9/ Before you send your letter or memo, make sure it is crystal clear what you want the recipient to do.

Put yourself in the reader's shoes and identify exactly the next step they should take after reading.

Then, articulate those steps for them.

10/ If you want ACTION, don't write. Go tell the guy what you want.

Last and most importantly, writing is never a replacement for a targeted conversation.

Most messages should be conversations, especially ones that require action.

Boom, that's it!

Which one will you start using today? Let me know!

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: Most life advice sucks.
entries.postBody:
Most life advice sucks.

Platitudes don’t change your life.

Here’s the stuff that will.

17 rules for a life well lived:

1/ Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2/ Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3/ Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4/ Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5/ Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6/ Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7/ Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8/ Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9/ Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10/ Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11/ Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12/ Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13/ Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14/ Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15/ Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16/ Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17/ Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

The LinkedIn Conversion Playbook by The Creator Accelerator by Chris Donnelly
Get it here for FREE: https://bit.ly/4lLWLut

Most LinkedIn profiles are glorified CVs. 

They list job titles, but do nothing to convert visitors into customers.

Even top entrepreneurs are losing leads because their profiles don't sell. 

I've strategically optimised my profile to achieve three goals:

- Waitlist for Searchable
- Subscriptions to my newsletter
- Sign-ups for The Creator Accelerator.

The results have been huge:

✅ 210,000+ subscribers to my email list
✅ 5000+ people on the Searchable waitlist 
✅ 5 fully sold out Creator Accelerator cohorts (with another incoming...)

And now, I want to share exactly how I did that with you, free of charge. 

This information could potentially be worth millions...

If you implement it as soon as possible. 

These are the exact steps I used to turn 1000s of profile views into leads. 

Inside, you'll learn how to: 

✅ Write About sections that convert using the TRUST method
✅ Transform your experience into outcome-based credibility
✅ Turn your banner into a digital billboard that drives traffic
✅ Use the positioning formula that turns headlines into micro-pitches
✅ Create instant trust with your profile picture and brand consistency
✅ Follow the complete 30-day implementation roadmap with weekly milestones
✅ Implement the monthly optimisation system that prevents performance decay
✅ Build a strategic funnel flow that guides visitors from awareness to action

Your LinkedIn profile is either making or losing you money. 

There's no in-between. 

This playbook turns it into a powerful landing page.

♻️ Repost to help your network make the most of their LinkedIn presence.

entries.hook: Freelance Writing 101
entries.structural: List post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
Freelance Writing 101

In 2016, I was making $50,000 per year as a copywriter at an advertising agency downtown Chicago.

Then, I quit my job.

30 days later, I was making $20,000 per month as a freelance writer.

Here's my Ultimate Guide to writing & making money online:

Step 1: Master ONE type of writing

The biggest mistake freelance writers make is spreading themselves thin.

- Writing blogs
- Writing newsletters
- Writing website copy
- Writing marketing materials

This is inefficient.

Master one thing. Do nothing else.

Step 2: Charge per ASSET, not per hour.

Writers who charge per hour (or worse, per WORD) are killing themselves.

Your value is not the # of hours you spend. 

It's the result you deliver.

Master 1 type of writing. And charge for the outcome.

Ex: 1 newsletter = $X.

Step 3: Work for free

The ROI on free work is limitless.

I went from being a nobody to ghostwriting for mega millionaires and billionaires by working for free for them, first.

Prove yourself. Show you can deliver the result. Keep sharpening your skills.

The money follows.

Step 4: Don't wait for clients. Sell!

After you've done some free work and proven you can *do it,* don't sit around.

Take it upon yourself to sell your services.

DM people you want to work for. Cold email. Tap your network. Get busy.

Even offer to do the 1st one free.

Step 5: Upsell current clients

There are 2 ways to grow your freelancing business.

- More, new clients
- More revenue from current clients.

The latter is 10x easier.

Ex: if your clients pay you to write newsletters, you can ALSO offer them long-form newsletters (for $3x).

Step 6: Raise your prices

When I first quit my job, I was charging $100 per ghostwritten article.

Then $200

Then $500

Then $800

Today, I charge almost $3,000 per ghostwritten article.

You have no idea where your *ceiling* is. 

The better you get, keep raising your prices.

Step 7: Optimize for GREAT clients (not mediocre ones)

Ideal clients save you time.

Mediocre ones cost you time.

$1,000 from an ideal client is better than $3,000 from a mediocre one. 

Ignore the dollar amount and focus on EFFICIENCY.

You'll earn more & work less.

Step 8: Celebrate each and every win

Every time you work with a bigger client, let all new prospective clients know.

Every time you reach some new milestone, use that in your sales conversations.

Make it very clear this is what you do, day in and day out.

Step 9: Put all your learnings into a product

The long-term gift of performing a service is racking up YEARS of expertise you can then turn into a product.

I did this by collecting all my strategies for digital writing and putting them into a book titled The Art & Business of Online Writing.

And then expanded that book into a cohort-based program called Ship 30 for 30, teaching people how to start writing online.

This is how you productize yourself and your learnings.

entries.hook: A powerful idea I can't stop thinking about:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
A powerful idea I can't stop thinking about:

Investor vs. Borrower

When faced with a decision, you choose a character:

1. Investor: Long-term thinker who delays gratification; sacrifices short-term pleasure for long-term reward

2. Borrower: Short-term thinker unwilling to delay gratification; experiences short-term pleasure with long-term cost

The Investor makes an investment.

An investment compounds with a positive rate of return into the future.

That investment—whether financial, physical, relationship, or anything else—is worth much more at a date in the future.

Our future self cashes in on that value.

The Borrower takes out a loan.

The loan provides upfront pleasure, but comes with a catch: Interest!

The interest accrues on top of the value of the loan. Even at a low rate, the value to be repaid has ballooned at a date in the future.

Our future self is stuck with the bill.

Entrepreneur and investor Naval Ravikant refers to "uphill decisions" as the choice to take the more difficult path in the short-term.

His logic is that the path with more short-term pain is typically the one with the largest potential for compounded long-term gain.

The core principle is simple:

The best of life comes from investing: from making the decision to do something difficult now to enjoy rewards of the compounding later.

Most things that feel good now will feel bad later, and most things that feel bad now will feel good later.

I have observed a variety of applications of the Investor vs. Borrower idea:

• Time Decisions
• Relationship Decisions
• Health Decisions

Here are a few examples:

Time Decisions

Procrastination is the most common manifestation of the Borrower mindset.

When you procrastinate, you take out a loan in the form of free time now, but you pay for it later when your future self is stuck with the bill in the form of stressful last minute work.

Relationship Decisions

Difficult conversations are a classic example of upfront pain for long-term gain.

It's easier to be a Borrower, to put them off. This is a loan that accrues interest and eventually must be paid.

Time rarely solves anything. Invest now or regret it later.

Health Decisions

When you choose short-term pleasure—in the form of inactivity, social media doom scrolling, binge drinking, whatever—you are taking out a loan with a steep cost in the future.

You'll never regret being an investor with your physical and mental health.

The Investor vs. Borrower idea is one that I can't unsee in my own life.

When I'm faced with a key decision, I ask myself two questions:

• Am I an Investor or Borrower?
• What would an Investor do here?

My goal: To have a high ratio of Investor to Borrower behavior.

Important Final Note: My goal is not a 100% Investor mindset! That would leave no room for the fun short-term decisions that add texture to life.

***

Join 450,000+ others who receive these insights in my 2x weekly newsletter: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.hook: We've all been there: You're firing on all cylinders—inspired and motivated. Then, suddenly, you aren't. Things become very, very difficult.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
We've all been there: You're firing on all cylinders—inspired and motivated. Then, suddenly, you aren't. Things become very, very difficult.

You're in a rut.

Here's my strategy to get out of it:

Last week, I fell into a rut.

I didn't want to wake up at my normal hour, didn't want to travel, and didn't want to do the daily writing that I normally love.

Fortunately, I've developed an approach for these situations:

1. Stop Digging
2. Change Direction
3. Create Movement

Step 1: Stop Digging

"The most important thing to do if you find yourself in a hole is to stop digging." ― Warren Buffett

A lot of us are digging ourselves deeper without realizing it.

This happens in two ways:

Negativity: It's easy to be hard on yourself when you're in a rut.

The negativity is a self-fulfilling prophecy—you start to believe negative things about yourself and find evidence to confirm it.

Exhaustion: You're a car stuck in the mud.

Ambitious people assume that they can get the car out of the mud by revving the engine and propelling forward, but the tire spins faster and faster and the car digs itself deeper and deeper.

We're avoiding the rest we desperately need.

The first step to get out of a rut is to stop digging.

• Upgrade Your Mindset: Eliminate the negativity and cut yourself a break.

• Take Rest: Don't try to get out of it in an instant. Give yourself time and space to rest. Remember that "rest" comes in many different forms.

Step 2: Change Direction

There are all variety of factors that are outside of your control that may have contributed to you falling into a rut, but this step solely focuses on those within your control.

Look internally and externally at the controllables:

Internally: How am I complicit in creating the conditions that I don't want?

This question forces you to pause and look inward—to ask whether there are any actions, mindsets, or patterns through which you were contributing to the rut.

How can you change those conditions?

Externally: How is your environment negatively shaping this reality?

Most of the time, your environment contributes to your rut.

You may just need a change of scenery, or you may need to remove some people or things that are draining your energy and contributing to the cycle.

Step 3: Create Movement

If you wait to act until you feel motivated, you may never start. When in doubt, just start moving.

Movement —> Momentum —> Motivation

To start the cycle, all you have to do is manufacture movement. Then you can sit back and let it take you for a ride.

My favorite method for manufacturing movement: Minimum Viable Progress.

Avoid the tendency to think that the movement has to be perfect.

Just do a tiny bit. Anything above zero compounds.

Life is a long game that all comes down to allowing positive decisions to compound.

Simply staying in the game is the most important thing.

***

I write about these topics in my newsletter. Join 400K others who receive it: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The "always-on" culture is killing us.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
The "always-on" culture is killing us.

Your phone buzzes at 7 PM.

Your heart drops.
Dinner goes cold.
Family time gets interrupted.

We've normalized total insanity.

When did we decide that:

- Every message needs an instant reply
- Every email demands immediate attention
- Every notification deserves our time

Here's what I believe:

That "urgent" message? It can wait.
That "ASAP" request? It's not life-or-death.
That after-hours email? Tomorrow exists.

Don't sacrifice your mental health because of someone else's poor planning.

You're a human being.
Not a productivity machine.

The real urgency isn't in responding to messages.

It's in escaping this situation.

Want to learn how I turned my expertise into a business on my own terms?

It started here on LinkedIn.

And I used this playbook: https://lnkd.in/eWqJGnvi

Independence starts with what you already know.

Get started.

Because your life is too short for someone else's urgency.

entries.creator: Lara Acosta
entries.hook: He was going viral every day. I was struggling to grow.
entries.postBody:
He was going viral every day. I was struggling to grow.

3 years ago, I connected with Charles. Viral sensation.

I was new to LinkedIn.
I was new to writing and storytelling.
I was new to networking (and shy at it). 

But Charles still engaged with me most weeks. 

Even shouted me out once on a post.
And drove around 1.5k followers in one day!

This was the ultimate confidence boost to keep going. 

Fast forward to today, I've grown to 250k followers here. 
Built 2x businesses on the way and made a new friend.

A big lesson I learned from him is to always support the small creators. No matter their following. 

Last week I got to thank him in person.
But now I want to to the same he did for me, for you.

I'm going to be supporting 10 new creators every week.

The ones who are your up and coming.
The ones who 'deserve' virality. 
The ones who you love. 

So please tag them below, or if its you repost ♻️ this. 

Excited to meet some of you soon!

entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

In my 20s, I built a $3,500,000 writing agency.

The entire business was built on ONE skill (steal this):

Cold outreach.

• No ads
• No huge audience
• No fancy website or funnel

80% of the 300+ clients I worked with came from cold outreach.

And I wrote for:

• Olympic athletes
• Silicon Valley founders
• Grammy award-winning musicians 

Learning how to prospect was 100x more valuable than actually being able to write!

And after teaching 1,500+ writers this system inside Premium Ghostwriting Academy...

I've just packaged everything up into The Writer Prospecting Blueprint.

Why?

Because I see too many writers (copywriters, ghostwriters, content writers, freelance writers) struggling to land high-ticket deals.

So I want to get this into as many hands as possible.

Inside you'll find:

• 4 ways to land clients (and why 3 of them waste your time)

• My complete 3-step outreach system from DM to sale

• Real examples of terrible vs excellent outreach messages

• The exact bottleneck analysis system to fix your outreach

• Scripts for booking calls and closing deals

And 3 videos breaking down every single framework.

Want access?

Comment "outreach" and I'll send it across (for free!)

And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "outreach" and I'll send it to you.

Writing online changed my life.

This simple habit has helped me:

• Make $10,000 as a ghostwriter
• Quit my corporate job on Wall Street
• And build an 8-figure digital business

It's why I'm convinced writing on the internet is the single most effective habit for personal growth and business building.

So, I'm on a mission to help 1,000,000 people start writing online.

To help you get started, I'm giving away 2 of my books for FREE:

1. The 5 Pillars of Digital Writing
2. The 22 Laws of Digital Writing

The frameworks packed inside these books are everything you need to start writing online.

They will help you:

• Create a daily writing habit
• Generate endless writing ideas
• Write irresistible hooks and headlines

And tons more!

And as an extra bonus, I'm including Nicolas Cole 🚢👻's The Art & Business of Ghostwriting.

Why?

Because ghostwriting is how I made my first $10,000 writing online.

And I want to help you use your Digital Writing skills to monetize as a ghostwriter.

Want access?

Like this post & comment "books"

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: I can't stop thinking about this question:
entries.postBody:
I can't stop thinking about this question:

Are you willing to sprint when the distance is unknown?

In 2021, Georgia Tech strength coach Lewis Caralla delivered this epic speech to the football team.

If it doesn't get you motivated, you may need to check your pulse...

He opens with a few harsh truths:

• Winning isn't loyal to you
• Winning doesn't care about you
• Winning doesn't care how sore you are
• Winning doesn't care how hard you work
• Winning doesn't care how much sleep you get

But it's his question that stuck with me:

Are you willing to sprint when the distance is unknown?

The willingness to sprint with no clear view of the finish line is rare.

It requires two things:

1. A deep belief in one's self
2. A deep belief in the mission

If you have 1 but not 2, you won't be able to do it.

If you have 2 but not 1, you won't be able to do it.

You need both.

In my observation, the greatest things in life are accomplished when you're willing to sprint when the distance is unknown:

The sprint to care for your loved ones in their time of need.

The sprint to build something meaningful.

The sprint to serve others and create positive ripples in the world.

Goal: Find those rare things in life that you're willing to sprint for when the distance is unknown.

That, to me, is the definition of winning.

"And why chase winning? Because the only thing that's guaranteed in life if you don't chase it is losing."

***

If you enjoyed this, share it with others and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The big "secret" about the people grinding 24/7 is that they'll regret it.
entries.postBody:
The big "secret" about the people grinding 24/7 is that they'll regret it.

They won't tell you that, of course.

And the comments will be filled with people defending a life of non-stop work.

They're going to change the world.
They enjoy spending all day at their job.
They are going to be wealthy beyond belief.

But the *actual* truth is a lot different.

How do I know?

Because I've lived it many times over.
Because most of my friends have done the same.

And no matter how much you enjoy work, how much money you make, what kind of impact you create, and how famous and adored you are...

Life sucks if you're neglecting your friends, family, and community.

So for those of you out there grinding because you think someone else's life is worth living...

Don't build someone else's idea of a great life.

Build your own.

I send one short note every Saturday on work, money, and a life built on purpose, to help you do that.

Join them here: https://buff.ly/9ABc8Yo

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The smartest people I know are the most teachable.
entries.postBody:
The smartest people I know are the most teachable.

That sounds backward.

You'd think the more you know, the less you need to learn.

But it works the opposite way.

The people who are really good at something?

They're constantly asking questions.
Admitting what they don't know.
Changing their minds when evidence changes.

The people who are stuck?

They already have all the answers.

I've been running my own business for almost 7 years now.

I still read stuff from people who are just getting started.

Still change my approach when something isn't working.

Still get things wrong every single day.

That's not a weakness.
That's part of the game of entrepreneurship.

The moment you decide you've figured it out is the moment you stop growing.

And in a world that changes this fast, standing still is the same as moving in reverse.

Be the person who's always learning.

Not because you're insecure about what you know.
Because you're curious about what you don't.

I write a weekly newsletter to 180K+ entrepreneurs who want to continuously get better.

Every week I share what's working, what's not, and what I'm still figuring out.

As a bonus, you'll also get my 2026 LinkedIn growth guide free when you sign up. 

It's the playbook I used to go from 2,000 to 815,000 followers.

Get it now → https://buff.ly/gqZIz2O

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: "Sorry, we're out of cups."
entries.structural: Question post, Quote + commentary, List post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
"Sorry, we're out of cups."

This was my interaction at Starbucks today.

And it left me with an important life & business reminder:

"Like, coffee cups?" I asked back to the barista.

"Yeah, we forgot to order more last week."

As I walked out without my caffeine fix, I couldn't help but think to myself:

How could Starbucks possibly run out of coffee cups?

Behind coffee *beans* the cups have to be the second most important thing on the supplies list, no?

Now, I wasn't totally surprised, because this Starbucks is usually a bit all over the place.

• Long lines
• Messy store
• Tons of employee turnover

But despite this, this morning they had a beautiful and well-organized "fall flavor" promotion.

Pumpkin spice this. Cinnamon latte that. Tons of big balloons and colors. 

Hours of prep work from the entire staff to turn the entire place into an orange Fall Frenzy.  

However, this grand promotion fell flat—but not for lack of excitement or interest from customers.

But because there were no cups—which meant no sales!

And that's what reminded me the importance of focusing on the absolute basics.

The shiny object of all of the balloons and decor.

The big promotion of all the pumpkin flavors.

All of this clearly took their attention away from the completion of the basics—ordering supplies. 

And without this strong foundation of the basics, all of the fancy add-ons fail.

And on my uncaffeinated, somber walk back to my apartment, I realized the importance of this reminder. 

Fitness, business, relationships—all of them flourish on top of a rock-solid, basic foundation. 

In fitness, you need to move more and eat less before worrying about advanced lifting techniques.

In business, you need to sell something people want before worrying about complex marketing funnels.

In relationships, you need to listen to other people and practice basic empathy before worrying about complex psychology. 

• Do the basics
• Do them every day
• Do them longer than the average person is willing

The rest takes care of itself.

(But it sure helps to be caffeinated...!)

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: My mom hired a young writer to sit down with my 94-year-old grandmother in India and document stories from her life.
entries.postBody:
My mom hired a young writer to sit down with my 94-year-old grandmother in India and document stories from her life.

They met weekly for two years.

The process brought my grandmother immense joy—the result will bring my family joy for years to come.

Everyone should do this...

My mom found the writer through an elderly care service in India. Her request was for someone who would record and transcribe stories from my grandmother.

The writer was a recent university graduate named Raika Sengupta.

The process began shortly after the pandemic lockdowns ended in India. Raika would visit with my grandmother every week.

Depending on my grandmother’s energy, they would meet for an hour or less. Each time, they would pick up where they left off on her life journey.

Raika would record the conversation and then transcribe the recordings—mostly in my grandmother’s own words, with some stylistic improvements where necessary. My grandmother is an AMAZING storyteller, so the writing naturally flowed.

The weekly meeting became a beautiful ritual.

During a time when there wasn’t much to look forward to because of COVID lockdowns and a diminished social life, my grandmother looked forward to these sessions.

They made her feel important again—such a powerful thing at her age.

Once the writing was complete, my mother—a beautiful writer herself—took the pen on editing and converting the stories into a book. She worked with my grandmother on the flow and filling in the gaps. They added chapters on special people in her life—siblings, friends, etc.

Then came the real fun…

We wanted to add photos to the book, so the whole family got involved. This week in India, I got to sit with my mom, dad, and grandmother and go through old photos to include. We uncovered some real gems. It was beautiful.

The book is almost complete. We plan to have copies printed to present to her on her 95th birthday. We will have enough copies made so that all of her family can have a copy to remember her by when she is gone.

I really believe everyone should do this. One of the saddest things is seeing our grandparents feel they are no longer “important” to anyone.

The process reminded my grandmother how much she is loved and how much she still has to offer the world.

***

I’ll write about the process and what we learned in a future newsletter. Join 250K others here: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

This letter hit me hard...

Every single person you encounter is fighting a battle that you know nothing about.

Your one tiny action can create powerful ripples that extend far beyond yourself.

Create more ripples.

For anyone out there battling in silence, you’re not alone.

We’re all in it together.

Lift someone up this week for no reason at all.

“I hope you win the war you tell no one about.”

♻️ Repost to help your network communicate better and follow Sahil Bloom for more.

entries.hook: Reminder: Forcing humans to sit still all day is foolish. Recess is sacred—for children and adults alike!
entries.postBody:
Reminder: Forcing humans to sit still all day is foolish. Recess is sacred—for children and adults alike!

This brain scan is from a study where researchers studied the effects of a 20-minute walk on the cognitive performance of a group of children. Reading comprehension performance was significantly better after exercise. Spelling and arithmetic performance were better, though the results were not statistically significant.

Researchers concluded:

“Single, acute bouts of moderately-intense aerobic exercise (i.e., walking) may improve the cognitive control of attention…and further supports the use of moderate acute exercise as a contributing factor for increasing attention and academic performance.”

A simple challenge for all of you: This weekend, go for a 30-minute tech-free walk.

• No phone
• No music
• No podcasts
• No articles
• No audiobooks

Just you, your thoughts, your gratitude, and the fresh air. A simple reset that will change your life. I guarantee it.

***

Join 250K+ others who get these insights in my 2x weekly newsletter: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

I asked 430,000 people for their favorite book of 2025.

These 12 books showed up the most often:

(So they must be worth reading over the holidays!)

https://a.co/d/dFsJuL0

2. The Art of Spending Money by Morgan Housel

Throughout December, I asked my audience for their 2025 highlights: favorite podcasts, newsletters, places, etc.

I'm curating these into "The Best of 2025" Notion library, which I'll release soon.

Follow me so you know exactly when it drops!

entries.hook: I journal every single morning for at least 30 minutes.
entries.postBody:
I journal every single morning for at least 30 minutes.

I've been doing this since 2010.

But over the years, I've learned you can (very easily) overcomplicate the act of journaling.

Which is why now I only use 4 prompts:

Question 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

Question 2: "What am I working on?"

• What are my goals?
• What are my most important habits?
• What lessons am I focused on learning?

Question 3: "What's coming up?"

• What's around the bend?
• What can I start proactively processing through now?

Question 4: "What am I grateful for?"

• What has changed me for the better?
• What am I fortunate to have?
• What hard lessons do I not regret?

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

And I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 5 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.engagementHeader: **Likes outlier** — 873 reactions ≈ **6.56×** mean · **Shares outlier** — 29 shares ≈ **7.75×** mean
entries.hook: Over the past 8 years, I have written more than 5,000 articles online.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
Over the past 8 years, I have written more than 5,000 articles online.

Accumulating 100 million+ views.

But guess what?

I use the same 5 headline styles every single time:

-

1/ The List

Everyone says they hate listicles.

...and then they click on them.

Lists are an amazing way to organize information in a way that is skimmable for the reader.

The key is:

If you're going to write a "7 Ways To..." article, you better deliver on your promise.

Here are some of my most-viral list article titles:

• 20 Things Most People Learn Too Late In Life (5M+ views)

• 19 Tiny Habits That Lead to Huge Results (2M+ views)

• 20 Things Nobody Tells You About Growing Up (1M+ views)

Lists work when the content DELIVERS.

-

2/ The Story

The best stories compress tons of information.

As Winston Churchill said: "If I had more time, I would have written you a shorter letter."

The key to writing a Story headline is this:

Be CLEAR, not Clever.

Writers think in order for it to be a "story," the title needs to be something mysterious.

This is a myth.

Ex: one of my most viral stories ever was:

What I Learned Being Betrayed By My 6 Closest Friends Growing Up

You 100% know what you're getting.

-

3/ The Opinion

If you're going to have an opinion, don't say it whilst sitting on the fence.

Pick a side.

Plant your flag in the ground.

Stand for something.

If you want to be heard on the Internet (whether you like it or not), you need to be polarizing.

Writers tend to misunderstand this advice and think they need to get political.

Polarizing just means knowing who your content ISN'T for.

And doubling-down on who it IS for.

Avoid words like: 

• Maybe
• Might
• Sometimes

And use words like:

• Absolutely
• Always

-

4/ The Question

If you are writing to answer a specific question your reader has, consider stating their question in the headline.

Ex: one of my most viral Q&A titles ever was:

How Do You Reinvent Yourself? Use These 7 Tiny But Powerful Mental Shifts

The secret here is DO NOT ANSWER THE QUESTION IN THE HEADLINE.

Don't say:

Want To Sleep Better? Give Up Coffee, Netflix, And Twitter

Instead say:

Want To Sleep Better? Give Up These 3 Everyday Guilty Pleasures

You have to create a curiosity gap for the reader.

-

5/ The Framework

My favorite headline style of all.

Readers LOVE frameworks. It feels like you're handing them a package that says: "Do this, it's a shortcut."

Frameworks can be:

• How To guides
• Templates
• Exercises
• Etc.

But in a Framework headline, there are 3 big things you need to communicate:

• What's the name of the framework?
• Who is it for?
• And why is it so important?

Ex:

The Endless Idea Generator: A Simple 5-Step Process For Generating 100 Article Ideas In 30 Minutes

99% of viral content on the Internet fits within these 5 headline styles.

They are everywhere.

Look at the next news notification on your phone—I bet it fits one of these styles.

So, use what works.

And make it your own.

entries.hook: I have a degree in Fiction Writing.
entries.postBody:
I have a degree in Fiction Writing.

Back in school, my teachers used to say, "If you can write fiction, you can write anything."

I don't think that's true. Being a great fiction writer doesn't make you a great non-fiction writer. A lot of the skills don't carry over and apply.

Instead, I think: "If you can write sales copy, you can write anything."

Sales copywriting skills are universal.

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: There are 4 types of professional time.
entries.postBody:
There are 4 types of professional time.

What they are (and 3 tips to optimize yours):

The 4 types of professional time are as follows:

1. Management
2. Creation
3. Consumption
4. Ideation

Management Time is what most of us spend the majority of our professional lives in. Meetings, calls, presentations, email processing, team and people management.

Creation Time is what most of us scramble to fill into the gaps between Management Time blocks. Writing, coding, building, preparing.

Consumption Time is where new ideas for creation and growth are originally planted. Reading, listening, studying.

Ideation Time is where new ideas for creation and growth are cultivated and grown. Brainstorming, journaling, walking, self-reflecting.

Before you can make improvements to your balance of time, you need to understand your starting point.

Starting on a Monday, at the end of each weekday, color code the events from that day according to this key:

• Red: Management
• Green: Creation
• Blue: Consumption
• Yellow: Ideation

At the end of the week, look at the overall mix of colors on the calendar.

This simple exercise should give you a clear picture of what your current baseline mix of professional time looks like.

3 Tips for an Optimal Balance

Tip 1: Batch Management Time

Create discrete blocks of time each day when you will handle major Management Time activities. 1-3 email processing blocks per day. 1-3 call and meeting blocks per day.

The goal here is to avoid a schedule where the red bleeds out everywhere across every single day. We are trying to keep the Management Time windows as discrete as possible to create space for the other types of time.

Tip 2: Increase Creation Time

Creation is what propels us forward, with more interesting projects and opportunities. We all need more Creation Time in our days.

As you batch Management Time, carve out distinct windows for Creation Time.

Block them on your calendar. Don't check your email or messages during them. Focus on creation during your Creation Time.

Tip 3: Create Space for Consumption & Ideation

Consumption and Ideation are the forgotten types of time because we rarely create space for them, but they are critical to long-term, compounding progress.

History's most successful people have all made a practice out of creating space for reading, listening, learning, and thinking. We can draw a lesson from this.

To start, schedule one short block per week for Consumption and one short block per week for Ideation. Stay true to the purpose of the block. Own that before increasing the presence of these types of time in your schedule.

With these three tips in mind, you're well on your way to finding a more optimal balance across the four types of professional time.

Give them a shot and let me know what you think!

If you enjoyed this or learned something, follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future!

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: You can lose the job. Lose the business.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
You can lose the job. 
Lose the business. 
Lose the money.

But you 𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗼𝘁 afford to lose yourself.

So why do so many people ignore the one thing they'll live with forever?

Themselves.

I see people do it all the time:

They'll spend 10 hours grinding at work.
Zero minutes asking "Who am I becoming?"

Plan their weeks down to the minute.
Haven't thought about their life in years.

That was 2018 me.

I thought "winning" meant optimizing everything around me:

- The ideal productivity system
- The perfect morning routine
- Bigger title, more money
- Crazy company growth

Meanwhile, I wasn't living my life mission.

That's when it hit me:

The only asset that matters is the person you become.

So I started treating myself like the #1 project that matters.

My whole life changed after that.

Most people invest in everything except themselves.

They'll drop $5k on a watch but won't spend $50 for self-improvement.

That's backward.

Start with you.
Everything else follows.

Interested in following a similar path?

I documented everything I learned quitting my job in 2019, then going from $0 to $10.3M in 6 years.

(without losing myself in the process)

It's a 100% free read.
↓
https://lnkd.in/eABypvdg

Is today the day you go all in on yourself?

entries.postBody:
Unpopular truth.


***

If we haven't met, hi hello... I'm Codie Sanchez.

I run Contrarian Thinking, an investment and advisory firm with communities where we teach people how to buy boring businesses, and build them into enduring, profitable businesses, to scale your wealth.

We're not about chasing hype. We're about laundromats, car washes, gyms, manufacturing shops, landscaping companies — the "unsexy" stuff that cashflows instead of cash burns.

We've taught 9,000+ operators, investors, and dealmakers business buying. And our students now run circles around many private equity shops.

Come learn how we buy, build, and scale companies the Contrarian way. Let's make you money, while we take back Main Street.

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: This clip of Steve Jobs is incredible...
entries.postBody:
This clip of Steve Jobs is incredible...

It has a lesson every single one of us needs to hear:

When Jobs was 12, he cold-called Hewlett-Packard co-founder Bill Hewlett and asked for spare parts.

It led to a summer internship and further stoked a curiosity that never went away.

An important reminder: Closed mouths don’t get fed.

If you want something—and you’ve done the work—ask for it.

• Worst case—you’re told no.
• Best case—it’s yours.

The old adage to sit back and wait for good things to happen is terrible advice.

A little push goes a long way.

And if you’re told no today, that’s NOT a no forever.

Just keep showing up.

Never bet against a person who just keeps showing up.

Enjoy this? Share it with your network ♻️ and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in the future.

📌 Interested in self-improvement? Download my free e-book of 50+ life hacks here: https://lnkd.in/edxXi82t

entries.creator: Codie Sanchez
entries.hook: "Our deepest fear is not that we are inadequate.
entries.postBody:
"Our deepest fear is not that we are inadequate.

Our deepest fear is that we are powerful beyond measure.

It is our light, not our darkness that most frightens us. We ask ourselves, 'Who am I to be brilliant, gorgeous, talented, fabulous?' Actually, who are you not to be? You are a child of God.

Your playing small does not serve the world. There is nothing enlightened about shrinking so that other people won't feel insecure around you."

– Marianne Williamson

Another wonderful quote that rings in my head whenever fear starts to creep in.

I hope it gives you some reassurance today.

If it does, feel free to share for others to find too ♻️

entries.hook: On the last day of the month, I do a self check-in.
entries.postBody:
On the last day of the month, I do a self check-in.

3 simple questions (that may change your life):

1. What really matters in my life right now and am I dedicating the necessary energy to it?

When you're in the trenches, it’s easy to lose sight of the important (and focus on the urgent). This question cuts through the noise.

2. Are my current systems and habits aligned with my long-term goals?

Assess the quality of your daily systems and whether they are creating the appropriate momentum.

3. What do I need to cut from my life to operate more efficiently?

These might be:

• Activities that are draining my energy
• Tasks I’m dreading completing
• Tasks I should be delegating
• Toxic habits or relationships.

Ruthlessly eliminate what's holding you back.

I write the answers in my physical notebook. The ritual takes ~30 minutes and creates an opportunity for monthly reflection and course correction.

It has been an invaluable practice since I started doing it 24 months ago.

“We do not learn from experience...we learn from reflecting on experience.” ― John Dewey

A failure to reflect will eventually manifest as a failure to grow.

Experience + Reflection = Growth

Join me this week and give it a shot—you won’t regret it!

***

Enjoy this? Share it with your network and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future!

I write about these topics for 700,000+ subscribers. Join them here: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: How to live a life of freedom and happiness.
entries.postBody:
How to live a life of freedom and happiness.

The Surfer Mentality:

When a surfer gets up on a wave, they enjoy the present moment, even though they know with certainty that the wave will eventually end. They fully enjoy THIS wave, with the wisdom and awareness that there are always more waves coming.

There are several other applications of the metaphor:

• The surfer knows that they don't have to ride every single wave that comes their way. They have the freedom and the power to choose which waves to take, and which waves to let pass by.

• The surfer knows that 90% of the time they won't be riding any wave, but just paddling and waiting. They know that sometimes there will be long, painful periods between the waves. Sometimes so long that they may start to wonder whether there will ever be a good wave again. They are aware that patience and proper positioning is all that matters.

• The surfer knows that the only way to live is by putting themself out there in the water. You can't catch any waves sitting on the shore. There's always a risk of getting crushed by a wave, but the rewards are worth it.

• The surfer knows that sometimes a wave will crash down on them and hold them underwater. They know that they may come up for air only to be hit by another wave crashing down. They are able to weather these hits with a stoic awareness that remaining fluid will serve them better than stiffening to fight the waves.

The Surfer Mentality has broad-based applications across all of our lives.

Here are my 5 takeaways you should steal:

1. Enjoy Your Next Wave: The next time you find yourself riding a positive wave, enjoy it! Don't worry about how long it will last, when it will end, or whether you will ever ride such a beautiful wave again. Just slow down and appreciate where you are in the present. Trust that there will be more waves in the future.

2. Be Strategic About Positioning: There's a tendency to passively wait between waves—but there's so much you can do in those liminal moments to set yourself up for a better ride. Think about your positioning and how you can make tweaks or changes that will put you in an advantageous spot when a wave does come.

3. Pass on More Waves: When you're young, you want to ride every single wave. But when you catch the first wave that comes your way, you may miss the better one that came right after it. Learn to pass on more waves and wait for the right one.

4. Always Get in the Surf: There's nothing good that happens sitting on the shore. Even if it's scary, get in the water.

5. Be Like Water: When you get crushed by a wave, don't resist the water, be like the water. With grief in particular, it's ok to just be and experience it rather than trying to fight it. Let it come over you and trust that it will diminish with time.

So as you approach the days ahead, think about adopting the Surfer Mentality: Enjoy every ride while it lasts!

If you enjoyed this, follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future.

entries.hook: A simple monthly ritual that will change your life in 2024.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
A simple monthly ritual that will change your life in 2024.

The Think Day:

In the 1980s, Microsoft founder Bill Gates began an annual tradition he called the Think Week.

He would seclude himself in a remote location, close all communication, and spend an entire week dedicated to reading, learning, and thinking.

The radical approach became a core part of his process:

"Think Week is a time when I can be creative and push my own thinking. It's a time to step outside the day-to-day demands of my job and really focus on the big picture." - Bill Gates

I first read about the Think Week years ago and immediately knew I wanted to give it a shot.

I knew I didn't have an entire week to dedicate to it (early career demands, family priorities, etc.), but I figured I could adapt something with a similar vision.

The Think Day was my creation:

Pick one day each month (or quarter) to step back from your day-to-day professional demands.

• Seclude yourself (mentally or physically)
• Shut off notifications on your devices
• Put up an out-of-office response

The goal is to create space to spend the entire day reading, learning, journaling, and THINKING.

By doing this, you create the free time to zoom out, open your mind, and think creatively about the bigger picture.

My essential tools for the Think Day:

• Journal and pen
• Books/articles I've been wanting to read
• Secluded location (at home, Airbnb, or outside)
• Thinking question prompts to spark my mind

6 thinking question prompts I've found useful:

1. How can you do less, but better?

2. Are you hunting antelope (big important problems) or field mice (small urgent problems)?

3. What are your strongest beliefs? What would it take for you to change your mind on them?

4. What are a few things that you know now that you wish you knew 5 years ago?

5. What actions were you engaged in 5 years ago that you cringe at today? What actions are you engaged in today that you will cringe at in 5 years?

6. What would your 80-year-old self say about your decisions today?

I aim for an 8-hour window (typical 9-5 day) split into 60-minute focus blocks with walks in between. The walks open your mind to more creative thinking (don't neglect them!).

You have to slow down to speed up.

In a world obsessed with speed, the benefits of slowing down are extensive:

• Restore energy
• Notice things you missed
• Be more deliberate with action
• Focus on the highest leverage opportunities

The Think Day can be a secret weapon for you in 2024. Give it a shot and let me know what you think.

***

Enjoy this? Share the post with your network and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in the future.

I first shared this idea in my free newsletter. Join 650,000+ others who received it here: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.postBody:
In 5 years, my little business has made $15,000,000.

And it runs on just $8,215/month.

Here are the 7 most powerful no-code tools in my tech stack:

In 2020, I was ghostwriting for:

• CEOs
• Executives
• Best-selling authors

And more.

I used years of writing experience to build a writing education business that today is doing 7-figs a year.

Here are the top 7 no-code tools that made it all possible:

1. ConvertKit ($1,179/mo)

The heart of our operation. Handles all email marketing:

• Automations
• Broadcasts
• CRM

We used this to grow from 0 to 125k subscribers, which scaled perfectly with us.

One of the most important tools in our arsenal.

2. Stripe (2.9% + $.30 per transaction)

By far the best payment system for digital writers.

• $15M+ processed
• Zero issues

It just works. No reason to use anything else.

For beginners, this is essential—start collecting payments from day one.

3. Carrd ($19/yr)

Every landing page we create starts here.

We buy stand-alone domains and host opt-ins that convert like crazy.

• It's fast
• Cheap
• And looks clean

No coding required.

It's what got us to $1M before we even needed more complex solutions.

4. Airtable ($1,134/mo)

The single most important database in our business.

It's our internal CRM and stores data in one central hub.

Every product, lead, and customer lives here.

If ConvertKit is the heart, Airtable is the brain of our operation.

5. Zapier (~$749/mo)

The ultimate glue that holds everything together.

Connects Stripe → Airtable → ConvertKit → Everything else.

• Easy to use
• Automates 100k+ tasks/month
• Replaces at least 5+ full-time assistants

This is how we scale without hiring an army.

6. ChatGPT ($20/mo)

Our go-to AI writing assistant.

• Analysis 
• Research
• Brainstorming

It's my best thought partner that I constantly spar with.

Every team member uses it daily to enhance their writing, not replace it.

7. Substack (Free or 10% of paid subs)

Hosts my personal + Write with AI paid newsletter (~$400k/yr).

The built-in referral engine is a massive growth lever.

For simple newsletters with monetization potential, nothing beats it.

This is the perfect platform for beginners.

That's it!

At scale, seemingly expensive tools become incredibly cheap relative to the revenue they generate.

📌 If I were starting from zero today, I'd use just 3 tools:

• ConvertKit for email
• Carrd for landing pages
• Stripe for payments

Make your first $1. 

Everything else comes after that.

I could build this writing education business because I've been paid to practice my writing for years.

How?

By ghostwriting.

It's the easiest online business to start (and way faster than building an audience or selling digital products).

Want help landing your first high-paying client?

Get started for free here: https://lnkd.in/e9vWpvkk

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: The easiest way to 10x your writing:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
The easiest way to 10x your writing:

Formatting — here's why:             

Formatting makes your work:

• Easier to skim
• Easier to quote & share
• And easier for readers to “remember” and repeat to their friends

So here's my 4-step formatting framework (this will change your life as a writer): 

Step 1: Start every section with a single-sentence opener.

When you open each area of your work with a single sentence, you:

• Keep readers interested
• Make things easier to read
• Subconsciously encourage them to continue

Here are the 6 most effective openers:

• Open with a moment in time
• Open with a vulnerable statement
• Open with a controversial opinion
• Open with a weird, unique insight
• Open with a thought-provoking question
• Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence

These are my go-to proven ways of hooking the reader.

--
Step 2: Always look for opportunities to turn long paragraphs into bulleted lists.

If you are:

• Listing anything, ever, or,
• Rambling off a dozen quick examples, or,
• Making a series of distinct points to drive home a point

...make it into a bulleted list.

(Like this!)

--
Step 3: Where are your subheads?

Subheads are bolded sentences that help the reader more easily follow your line of thinking.

• They split the page up evenly
• They offer big, bolded “milestones”
• They help the reader identify where they’re going

Subheads give clarity.

--
Step 4: Use the 1/3/1 writing rhythm.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

You can also use:

• 1/4/1
• 1/5/1
• 1/2/5/2/1
• Etc.

The key to injecting skimmability into your writing is alternating the length of sentences and sections.

--
In all your formatting choices, your primary concern should always be:

"How can I make this easier for the reader?"

• Is a bulleted list easiest? Do that.
• Is a step-by-step guide easiest? Do that.
• Is a series of small, rapid-fire sections easiest? Do that.

Your North Star is the reader's enjoyment.

---
📌 Looking to start writing online?

Here’s a FREE 13,000-word Ultimate Guide + email course with everything you need to:

• Build a daily writing habit
• Generate hundreds of ideas
• Start going viral with ease

Download it here: https://lnkd.in/eBSCxsqR

entries.hook: 19 brutal truths about going your own way in life:
entries.postBody:
19 brutal truths about going your own way in life:

1. Nobody will believe in you until you believe in yourself.

2. The right way for someone else is the wrong way for you. (And the right way for you is the wrong way for someone else.)

3. It's only a mistake if you don't extract a lesson.

4. You can't change what you aren't aware of.

5. It's not about what rewards you receive, it's about who you become.

6. Greatness is a tiny decision you make each day.

7. Focus is the path of least resistance. (Simplicity is velocity.)

8. The world says you can't until you can.

9. What you want is who you are.

10. Actions speak louder than words, and results speak louder than aspirations.

11. Those who don't understand you don't understand themselves.

12. Other people's expectations are the heaviest burdens of all. (Put them down.)

13. Differentiation costs acceptance.

14. Errors are just misdirected efforts.

15. Wondering is a window, not a door.

16. Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

17. You can only truly know what you have experienced.

18. Fulfillment is a byproduct of self-acceptance.

19. The journey is the joy.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: Want to write a 60,000-word book this October?
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
Want to write a 60,000-word book this October?

Here's my (dead-simple) 3-step process:

The secret to writing a book?

Math.

• 10 chapters at 5,000 words each
• Each chapter has 5 to 7 sub-questions
• These sections are then only 700 to 1,000 words long

Each section is the length of a blog post or a newsletter.

So your book is a series of shorter posts bolted together.

Add in an introduction and conclusion, and you can easily hit 60,000 words.

Now, for the process.

Before you start writing, you need to have two items in place:

1. Your title
2. Your outline

If you don't have these, you don't know what you're writing about.

Now, here are the 3 steps to write each chapter:

Step 1: Prep the Chapter on the page

1. Write out the overarching question for the chapter
2. List out the sub-questions/topics for the question

With the chapter prepped, writing becomes a fill-in-the-blanks exercise (and far less intimidating).

Step 2: Expand each sub-question/topic

For each of these, ask yourself:

"What does the reader need to understand about the sub-question and overarching chapter question?"

The best way to think about this?

Use the 10 magical ways of expansion.

Any piece of writing can be expanded in 10 ways:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

Mix and match these to answer the sub-questions and overarching question of the chapter for the reader.

Step 3: Make a round of edits

Don't get bogged down in this.

Some editing pointers:

• Look out for repetition
• Edit for flow and readability
• Do a final read-through out loud to pick up any clunky parts

And just like that, you wrote a 5,000-word chapter.

Repeat 11 more times & your book's finished.

Before I learned this framework, it took me 4 years to write my first book.

Since then, I've published 10+ books.

And I made a 6-figure living while doing it.

How?

By ghostwriting.

It's the fastest way to monetize your writing. And right now, the easiest way to get started is ghostwriting on LinkedIn.

Want to land high-paying writing clients?

Get started here: https://lnkd.in/eMKFExNM

entries.hook: 17 things successful people tell themselves:
entries.postBody:
17 things successful people tell themselves:

1. Today is a great day to build.

2. No one is responsible for my success besides me.

3. I welcome the learning process with open arms.

4. Everyone was once a beginner.

5. Mastery over my craft is the highest accolade.

6. I will not let someone else define my self-worth.

7. To unlock the upside, I must face the risk of the downside.

8. I need to be patiently impatient and have a bias toward action.

9. The enemy of long-term compounding is short-term results.

10. Wealth is built over decades, not days.

11. I have no competition because I am only competing against myself.

12. The world rewards those who do what others do not.

13. I trust myself and my convictions.

14. Every failure is an opportunity to learn.

15. Every success is an opportunity to start again.

16. Forward is the only option. There is no going back.

17. I own my shortcomings—and leverage them to my advantage.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.
entries.postBody:
The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.

Or, you could spend less than $250 and read these 8 legendary books on marketing, positioning, and copywriting:

1. Play Bigger by Christopher Lochhead, Al Ramadan, and Kevin Maney,

Category Kings win 76% of the category economics.

Everyone else fights over the remaining 24%.

You cannot be a legendary marketer in today's day and age without understanding Category Design.

--

2. Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon

The best way to grow your business isn't "more customers."

It's more Superconsumers.

Supers spend 30-70%+ more than the average consumer, and buy more products/services more often.

Win them, and you win the game.

--

3. Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout

One of the most timeless marketing books ever written, Positioning is the art of creating new space in the customer's mind for your product/service/offering.

No differentiated position, no meaningful company.

--

4. What They Don't Teach You at Harvard Business School by Mark H. McCormack

There's the way the world works "in theory." (School)

And then there's the way the world actually works.

This book is a wake-up call: relationships drive everything.

5. How To Win Friends & Influence People by Dale Carnegie

One of the first books to straddle the line between business and self-help.

This is a guide on communication, relationship-building, and creating your own definition for success.

--

6. Think & Grow Rich by Napoleon Hill

The fact this book isn't required reading in MBA programs blows my mind.

In order to become successful, Hill says you must position your Chief Aim in your mind's eye.

And obsessively pursue it.

--

7. The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson

Curated wisdom from Silicon Valley investor & founder of AngelList, Naval Ravikant, this is one of my favorite "business" books ever.

It is a guide for success while also acknowledging the flaws of the game.

--

8. The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel

Finally, before you graduate and enter "the real world," you should probably know...

Getting rich is easy.

Staying rich, and not behaving poorly with money, is the hard part.

--

9. BONUS: The Art & Business of Online Writing by Me 

The single most valuable skill you can have in the world today is the ability to communicate clearly, at scale.

Aka: writing online.

entries.hook: 7 reframes you NEED as an employee if you want to become an owner:
entries.postBody:
7 reframes you NEED as an employee if you want to become an owner:

1. You'll never be a good entrepreneur if you can't solve problems at your job.

2. Anyone can complain about what's wrong - leaders brainstorm how to make it right. When you highlight a problem, ALWAYS have potential solutions ready. Reduce others' work to a simple 'yes' or 'no' exercise.

3. Crabs in a bucket pull each other down trying to escape. It's the same at your job... Instead of obsessing about others' success, ask what YOU can do to win. Then help others do the same.

4. Re-frame demands to align with others' incentives. Don't just ask for a raise, say, "If I can make the company 25% more, I'd like an X% cut of it."

5. Almost every business owner has a ledger somewhere with one thing... All the money people never paid them back (and probably never will). Find & solve evergreen problems like this.

6. Highlight team wins, even if you drove them. Own failures, even if others caused them. Forgo short-term pleasure for an unassailable long-term reputation.

7. Become too valuable to live without. Ask questions. Shadow pros across the company. Take on new responsibilities. Become the person your manager negotiates with vs the other way round.

Follow me Codie A. Sanchez for more like this. Feel free to share if this resonated ♻️

entries.creator: Codie Sanchez
entries.hook: Hard truth realized as a CEO.
entries.postBody:
Hard truth realized as a CEO.

Those who work from home do a lot less work at home.

But it's more than productivity.

I think WFH is one of our greatest disservices to a new generation of adults.

The average person will spend 90,000 hours with coworkers over their lifetime.

Do you think 90,000 zoom meetings is going to improve your mental health?

We could solve most of youth loneliness if we got back in in-person startups, building things. A small group with sleepless nights and shared goals is a powerful thing.

You are sad, lonely, failing?

Go to the office. Remote work was a scam.

entries.hook: People think they need a business plan.
entries.structural: Question post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
People think they need a business plan.

They don't.

People think they need permission to pivot their career.

They don't.

People think they need approval to build something new.

They don't.

The biggest lie we tell ourselves?

"I need to wait for the right moment."
"I need someone to tell me it's okay."
"I need more experience first."

Here's the truth:

You already have everything you need to start.

The work isn't waiting for permission.
The work is doing the thing.

How many people do you know who spend months planning instead of building?

How many people ask everyone else what they should do instead of just trying?

The most successful business owners I know didn't wait.

They started messy.
They figured it out along the way.
They gave themselves permission to begin.

If you don't know how to start, here's my 111-lesson playbook for building a profitable online business: https://lnkd.in/gBnHYrjW

Image credit: tiffinikilgore (Instagram)

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Only foolish people dismiss hard work as "luck."
entries.postBody:
Only foolish people dismiss hard work as "luck."

"They're lucky they can work from home."
"They're lucky their business took off."
"They're lucky they have that lifestyle."

Luck?

Behind every "overnight success" is years of work.

Pitching dozens of clients before landing the first one.

Failing at multiple business ideas before finding one that works.

Learning how to market, sell, and deliver value to people.

Reinvesting every dollar back into growing instead of buying stuff.

That's not luck. 
That's called persistence.

Most people see the result, not the process.

Luck is when preparation meets opportunity.

And you create your own luck by showing up consistently, learning from failures, and pushing on when things get hard.

The only difference between people who "get lucky" and people who don't?

The lucky ones keep going when everyone else gives up.

Ready to create your own business luck?

Here's a guide I wrote that breaks down how I built mine from $0 to 8-figures in 5.5 years.

Read it free: https://lnkd.in/ecdsCRDm

Image credit: impossibleachievers

entries.hook: The simple habit I used to lose 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and build a 7-figure writing business:
entries.postBody:
The simple habit I used to lose 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and build a 7-figure writing business:

Journaling.

Over the last 7 years, I have:

• Journaled every day
• Tried over 1,000 questions
• Tested every app, pen, & notebook

I can point every good thing in my life back to this simple, daily practice.

With it, I have:

• Started writing online
• Made my first $10,000 on the internet as a ghostwriter
• Quit my full-time job on Wall Street to go all in on entrepreneurship

And over the last few years, I've narrowed down these 1,000 questions to 12 journal prompts I use on a daily, weekly, and monthly basis.

And now I've put them together in my Ultimate Guide To Journaling.

Inside, you'll find:

• 12 tried-and-test journal prompts
• Prompts to get you unstuck and motivated
• 2 simple prompts I use to generate 100s of content ideas

And tons more!

Want access?

✅ Like this post
✅ Comment "JOURNAL"
✅ And send me a connection request if you haven’t yet (so I can DM you)

I’ll send it over ASAP.
