This experiment on luck will blow your mind...

In 2003, Dr. Richard Wiseman conducted a series of simple experiments to explore why some people seem to consistently "get lucky" while others struggle with bad luck their whole lives.

He placed ads requesting participants for a study on luck—specifically asking for people who considered themselves very lucky or very unlucky.

In one experiment, each participant was given a newspaper and asked to count the number of photographs inside it.

The unlucky group averaged 2 minutes to complete the task, while the lucky group averaged just seconds.

Why the striking difference?

On page 2 of the newspaper, there was an enormous bold font print that read, "Stop counting, there are 43 photographs in this newspaper."

The lucky people had seen the writing, stopped, and responded accordingly to end the timer.

The self-identified unlucky people had missed it (or mistrusted it) and taken far longer to count.

This finding grew into a consistent theme across the body of research:

The lucky people's daily behaviors, habits, and actions led them to come across "chance" opportunities, while the unlucky people seemed to miss them.

Both groups had equal access, but the lucky group saw what the unlucky group tended to miss.

This points to a concept I often refer to as "luck surface area" in my writing:

The idea is that each of us has a surface area on which lucky events can strike.

There are a few baseline factors out of our control:
• Where you are born
• Who you are born to
• "Acts of God"

Beyond these, the size of our luck surface area is within our control.

In Dr. Wiseman's study, the lucky people understood this:

• They noted that they often took alternate routes to and from work so that they would meet new people and see new things.

• They bounced back from seemingly negative encounters and maintained a positive outlook for the future.

• They talked about unique strategies for talking to different groups of people at parties.

The luckiest people have engineered an enormous luck surface area.

Expand yours in two ways:

1. Add Pro-Luck: Pro-luck includes all the actions, behaviors, and people that expand your luck surface area. Getting out and meeting new people, sharing your thoughts and ideas publicly, and sending more cold emails and DMs are all common sources of pro-luck. People who encourage you to think bigger are another common source.

2. Remove Anti-Luck: Anti-luck includes all the actions, behaviors, and people that shrink your luck surface area. Pessimism and "blinders" are two common sources of anti-luck. People who tell you to be realistic are another common source.

Lesson: Expand your luck surface area to create your own luck.

***

If you enjoyed this, share the post with others and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future!

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Your brain is sick and tired of content.
entries.postBody:
Your brain is sick and tired of content.

It wants you to be creative.

Over the past 6.5 years, I've written:

- 3,559 LinkedIn posts
- 4,107 Tweets
- 789 Instagram posts
- 456 Threads
- 278 Substack notes
- 266 newsletter issues

And here's what I've learned:

1. The more you create, the happier you are:

On the days I'm sharing my creations, my mood is incredibly elevated. There's nothing better than learning something and sharing it with other people.

2. The more you consume, the more pessimistic you become:

Whenever I go down a rabbit hole on LinkedIn or X, I get bummed out. Culture wars, fighting, lack of empathy for our fellow humans. It's gross.

3. The longer you write, the better at your craft you become:

If you write every single day for a long time, you will eventually become a relatively decent writer. And there's no better way to communicate your thoughts.

4. The more you outsource creative work to AI, the dumber you become:

The more AI does your creative work, the more you forget how to be creative and original. You sound like everyone else. You blend in. You're not interesting.

TL;DR:

1. Create daily
2. Consume less
3. Write until you're good
4. Don't outsource creative

I teach 200,000+ creative entrepreneurs how to:

- Get attention
- Keep attention
- Monetize attention

I'll show you how when you join my weekly newsletter: https://buff.ly/iKAl3na

(→ You'll also get my free 2026 LinkedIn writing guide instantly.)

entries.hook: How to get more done in less time.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
How to get more done in less time.

The Eisenhower Matrix:

Dwight Eisenhower was known for his prolific productivity. His secret?

"What is important is seldom urgent and what is urgent is seldom important."

The Eisenhower Matrix is a 2x2 popularized by Stephen Covey in The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People.

Here's how it works:

The matrix forces you to differentiate between the urgent and the important to prioritize your time accordingly. An URGENT task is one that requires immediate attention to complete. An IMPORTANT task is one that contributes to your long-term mission or goals.

The four quadrants of the 2x2 grid:
• Important & Urgent
• Important & Not Urgent
• Not Important & Urgent
• Not Important & Not Urgent

Let's walk through each:

Important & Urgent

They require immediate, focused attention—but also contribute to your long-term mission or goals. These are "Do Now!" tasks. Manage these tasks.

Important & Not Urgent

These tasks are your compounders—they build long-term value in your life. These are "Decide" tasks. Spend more time on these tasks—plan the time to do deep work here. This is where you should try to spend most of your time and energy.

Not Important & Urgent

These tasks are the "beware" category—they can drain time and energy without contributing to your long-term goals. These are "Delegate" tasks. Spend less time here and try to delegate over time to people for whom the tasks will be important.

Not Important & Not Urgent

These are the time wasting tasks and activities that drain your energy and sap your productivity. These are "Delete" tasks. Spend less time here.

Use the Eisenhower Matrix to categorize your current tasks.

The Goal: Spend more time on the important tasks that contribute to your long-term values and goals.

In simple terms:
• Manage top-right
• Spend more time in top-left
• Waste less time in the bottom half

***

Join 200K others who receive my 2x weekly newsletter where I share actionable ideas to help you live a high performing, healthy, wealthy life: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.hook: I just made either the biggest mistake or biggest investment of my life...
entries.postBody:
I just made either the biggest mistake or biggest investment of my life...

It's called BizScout.

I just invested millions into it.

Tell me what you think...

THE IDEA:

We need a better marketplace that makes buying and selling a business so easy even your grandma can do it.

We take back Main Street by owning it.

But right now there is a 25 year old incumbent that owns this market, hasn't innovated in a decade, and still gets 10s of millions of views a month but makes business-buying... miserable.

What's different at BizScout (to start):

• ScoutSights: Quick validation of market price based on 300,000 historical listings (like Zillow's Zestimate).
• Curated listings: BizScout will have a more curated selection of listings, ensuring that you're only seeing high-quality, verified opportunities.
• Off-Market leads: Instead of paying $30k for institutional level off market deal tech, we do it for you.
• Buyer verification: BizScout will verify all paid users (who opt in) so brokers actually respond to you.

THE VISION:

[...Zillow CEO quote about real estate transparency, $11B company analogy...]

THE OPPORTUNITY

We buy the businesses instead.

• SMB owners are 65+ on average
• 75M baby boomers will retire by 2030
• 61% say they have no idea what to do with their business or how to sell

[...market gap analysis: investment banks don't want small messy deals, neither does PE...]

THE UNFAIR ADVANTAGE:

I am already one of the largest small business brokerages in the US.

We've seen confirmation of closing of more than $300M in business through our community.

I think we could make this thing work.

I guess we'll see...

Love for you all to check it out and give us feedback → BizScout.com

You'll be seeing lots more from us in the coming weeks.

entries.hook: This 30-second iPhone hack changed my life...
entries.postBody:
This 30-second iPhone hack changed my life...

Grayscale Mode removes the colors to make your phone immediately less appealing and addicting.

To turn it on, follow these steps:

(1) Settings
(2) Accessibility
(3) Display & Text Size
(4) Color Filters -> On
(5) Grayscale

If you want to be able to toggle it on and off easily, you can create a simple shortcut:

(1) Settings
(2) Accessibility
(3) Accessibility Shortcut
(4) Color Filters

If you triple-click the side button, you'll be able to toggle Grayscale on and off.

Since starting to use Grayscale Mode in January, I've seen my screen time drop and my focus rise.

For non-iPhone users…

Grayscale Mode on Google Pixel:

(1) Settings
(2) Accessibility
(3) Color & Motion
(4) Color Correction
(5) Grayscale

Grayscale Mode on Samsung Galaxy:

(1) Settings
(2) Accessibility
(3) Visibility Enhancements
(4) Color Correction
(5) Grayscale

Try it for a day. It's a massive life hack.

***

Join 500,000 others who receive these insights in my 2x weekly newsletter: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.hook: How to make progress (on big intimidating goals).
entries.postBody:
How to make progress (on big intimidating goals).

My 30-for-30 Approach:

I’ve spent a lot of time figuring out the best ways to create progress on big, intimidating goals.

My 30-for-30 Approach is what I’ve found that consistently works.

The structure is simple:

• 30 days
• 30 minutes per day

With this approach, three things happen:

1. Clarity

It provides clarity on whether you are physically and psychologically invested in the thing you want to improve at.

30 days of effort is a real commitment.

If you are half-in, you won’t want to take it on and commit to the 30 days.

It’s a commitment razor.

2. Removes Intimidation

30-for-30 is small enough that you can mentally take it on.

Pre-start self-intimidation is one of the biggest drivers of stagnation.

We make something too daunting, so we don’t take it on.

New habits and improvement initiatives can often feel that way.

3. Effective Compounding

30 days of 30 minutes per day is 900 total minutes of accumulated effort.

It will yield surprisingly significant results.

There’s almost nothing in the world that you won’t improve at if you spend 900 minutes of focused, dedicated effort on it.

30-for-30 works because it creates a marathon of short, manageable sprints.

It forces you to work like a lion, consistently.

Intensity + Consistency = Progress

What do you want to take on this month? Give my 30-for-30 Approach a shot and let me know what you think.

***

Enjoy this? Share it with your network and follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future.

The 30-for-30 Approach is one of the System Building Mental Models covered in my Annual Planning Guide. You can download it for free here: https://lnkd.in/eMKTiVVf

entries.hook: Your single greatest 21st-century superpower:
entries.postBody:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

Your single greatest 21st-century superpower:

Pursuing the inconvenient truth over the comforting lie.

So here are 19 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:

--

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

--

2. No one on Earth thinks about you as much as you think they do.

--

3. Successful people are neither smarter nor more creative than you. They are simply executing better.

--

4. Success comes from doing somewhat-monotonous things every day for years – but no one wants to hear that.

--

5. You know exactly what you should be doing, so stop chasing shiny objects. Instead, figure out why you aren't doing what you know you should be.

--

6. You can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation.

--

7. If you spent half as many hours taking action as you did thinking about taking action, you would be in a better spot.

--

8. 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity.

--

9. If you are reading productivity blogs, you are working on the wrong project.

--

10. You can't deposit the number of books you've read or followers you have in a bank account.

--

11. You said you would start months ago – the perfect time will never come. 

--

12. You have three finite resources: time, energy, and attention.Every time you waste them, they're gone forever.

--

13. You can complain about the game or you can learn the rules.

--

14. The market rewards not the best product, but the product who best positions themselves as the obvious solution.

--

15. If you're afraid to publish things online, you have an ego problem.

--

16. The people you look up to are not that special and are no more talented than you are.

-- 

17. If you did everything you said you'd do, your results would be 10x better than they are – so why aren't you doing them?

--

18. Being "busy" is a sign you lack leverage and are delegating poorly.

--

19. 99% of people would rather be uncomfortable and unhappy than uncertain.

-- 


That's it. Most people spend their lives avoiding these truths (and end up in a spot they don't want to be because of it).

Start pursuing these truths and watch your results accelerate overnight.

entries.postBody:
I made my first $10,000 online as a ghostwriter.

But if I started from zero today, I would:

• Start writing on LinkedIn
• "Pitch In Public" to land clients
• Use DMs to network with more clients

In other words, I'd become a LinkedIn ghostwriter.

Why?

Because the demand for ghostwriters on this platform is exploding.

So, to help you get started, I've created a 30-day roadmap to show you how to land your first (or next) LinkedIn ghostwriting client.

All for free.

Why am I giving this away?

I originally created this as a bonus for my paid coaching program. 

But the strategies are working so well that I want to help more people break into this growing (and profitable) industry.

Here's what you'll get inside the 30-Day LinkedIn Ghostwriter Roadmap:

• My 30-day roadmap with daily action steps
• 8 powerful AI prompts to accelerate your results
• Proven outreach templates for warm and cold leads
• My "Pitch in Public" framework to attract ideal clients
• Sales call script generator to close your first (or next) deal

Want access?

✅ Like this post
✅ Comment "roadmap"
✅ Send me a connection request (so I can DM you)

I'll send you the roadmap right away.

PS—This system works even if you have zero writing, sales, or AI experience.

I've designed it specifically for beginners who want to start earning good money as a LinkedIn ghostwriter in the next 30 days.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Observation / Contrarian take
entries.postBody:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting.

And before they know it, years have gone by and it's too late to change.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

The goal of The Monthly Review:

Block 60 minutes to slowly process the month.

It's easy to get caught up in the day to day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

And after trying 1000+ reflection questions, I chose each question for a specific reason.

Let's start with the first:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things that you will look back on with pride & fond memories at the end of the year.

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, our focus is on the negative. But this questions helps us celebrate all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

From there, you ask a more important question:

---
2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—because something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

And by constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By constantly iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

---
3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page in 2 columns and brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

The goal here is to identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

---
4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive *and* negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to *double down* on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to *stop doing entirely* that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

---
5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a quick list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

---
And just like that, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things throughout the month.

• Wins & milestones
• Realizations & decisions
• Things going well & things to improve

Then, at the end of the year, you collect these monthly reviews and distill the entire year.

entries.hook: The best career advice:
entries.postBody:
The best career advice:

You should either earn a lot or learn a lot.

People put a lot of weight into salary. 
But learning can be just as valuable in the long term...

Provided you use it to start earning later.  

Ideally, you will both earn and learn. 
If you are doing neither, quit. 

This piece of career advice will change every decision you make. 

Here are 9 others: 

1. Build a personal brand. ✅
↳ Shows personal drive and initiative. 

2. Overcome self-doubt by building confidence. 🙌
↳ Shift your mindset and start advocating for yourself. 

3. Embrace every role as a learning opportunity. 💡
↳ Strive for continuous growth and development. 

4. See failure as a chance to grow. 📈 
↳ Every obstacle is a stepping stone to something better. 

5. Be proactive and seek solutions. 🔧 
↳ Carve out a path based on your own goals. 

6. Take action to dispel anxiety. 🏃  
↳ Making progress kills procrastination. 

7. Work both smart and hard. 🧠 
↳ There are no shortcuts in work or life. 

8. Choose your opportunities wisely. ⚖️
↳ You can't (and shouldn't) say yes to everything. 
 
9. Networking is fundamental to success. 🤝
↳ If you want to get anywhere, you need other people. 

Most importantly: 
Learn from those who have been through it.  
They'll be more valuable to you than any degree. 

Do you agree?
Let me know ⬇️

♻️ Repost this to help your network grow.
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

P.S. If you're looking to grow and monetise your personal brand,
Check out the link below and book your spot for cohort 4 of CA:

https://lnkd.in/eJGiMAkr

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: You feel like a mess.
entries.postBody:
You feel like a mess.

But they think you're a machine.

The gap between how you see yourself and how the world sees you is massive.

Inside your head?

It is loud.
You are overthinking every move.
You are doubting every decision.
You feel like you are just making it up as you go.

But nobody else hears the noise.
They only see the signal.

They see you showing up.
They see you shipping work.
They see you building.

Here is the secret about high performers:

They don't have less doubt.
They just have more discipline.

They don't let "overthinking" turn into "stopping."

But if you want to silence the doubt for good?
Stop trying to do everything.

You are overwhelmed because you are chasing 50 different tactics.
You only need to focus on one.

In 2026, the highest leverage activity is building an audience.

It is the one asset that makes every other part of business easier.

When you have attention, you have options.
And when you have options, the anxiety disappears.

So stop guessing.
And start building.

I just finished the roadmap that shows you exactly how to do it.

It is called the "2026 Guide to LinkedIn Growth."

I'll send it to you as a free gift when you join 175,000+ entrepreneurs in my weekly newsletter.

You'll get the guide immediately, plus weekly strategies to:

• Build visibility
• Grow an audience
• Turn expertise into income

Get it all here: https://buff.ly/nto01ey

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: I lived through Crying CEO Guy.
entries.structural: Question post, Story post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
I lived through Crying CEO Guy.

I didn't take a mocking selfie. 
I didn't blast him in the comments.
I didn't go to his Instagram and make fun of him.

Want to know why?

Because humiliating people isn't my thing.

And it never will be.

Because I've done and said a lot of really stupid things over the course of my 41 years here on this planet.

And when people have gone out of their way to humiliate me, it sucks.

So a few years ago, I made it a habit to remember that even the silliest mistakes are made by someone's son or daughter, spouse, parent, or sibling.

I've found that to be a helpful thought to revisit when I feel like piling on.

Look...I obviously hope crying selfies don't become the norm.

But, I hope humiliating people on LinkedIn doesn't either.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office?
entries.postBody:
Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office? 
 
Or spend all day at your desk? 
 
Me too.  
 
Sure...I understand the camaraderie.  
 
And some things are more easily done in person than virtually. I get that. 
 
But here's something else I think: 
 
Success is not about long hours in the office. Or sitting at your desk from 9 to 5.  
 
It's about good ideas and sharp execution. 
 
And sometimes, the best ideas come when you're away from your desk.  
 
Like at the dinner table, on a run, or playing with your kids.  
 
Spending time with family isn't just nice, it helps us think better. 
 
Don't get stuck on the old idea that more hours at work means more success.  
 
Try something different.  
 
Spend more time living life, and you might find you're better at your job. 
 
Take a step back and think about this.  
 
Maybe a new way to measure success is in the quality of our ideas, and how we execute on them - not the quantity of our hours.  
 
Your desk isn't going anywhere, but your life is.

Build life-work balance. Not the other way around.

More here: https://lnkd.in/eukQAFD5
 
Repost ♻️ if you think being forced back to the office & desk is silly.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: I wasted years as a horrible writer.
entries.postBody:
I wasted years as a horrible writer.

Then I heard Naval Ravikant mention a 250-word essay full of the best writing mental models he swears by.

On The Tim Ferriss Show, Naval said this is his go-to resource for writing is:

"The Day You Became A Better Writer" by Scott Adams.

Here are the 4 most important mental models:

1. Keep it simple.

That's the #1 rule of concise and persuasive writing, according to Adams. 

• Remove adverbs
• Use shorter words
• Instead of "very happy" say "happy"

This is similar to Hemingway's rules for writing.

2. Hook the reader from the very first sentence.

Curiosity is the crack cocaine of the writing world.

Hook your readers, and they'll follow you anywhere.

• Study David Ogilvy: https://lnkd.in/eyB4Ahcq

• And use these 6 proven sentence openers: https://lnkd.in/eqrAEmT9

3. Use short sentences.

"Readers aren't as smart as you'd think."

Just one thought per sentence. Readers process info better when it's bite-sized and easily digestible.

Avoid:

 • Jargon
 • Flowery language
 • Complex sentences

Be clear, and you'll be clever.

4. Subject before action.

"The boy hit the ball" not "The ball was hit by the boy."

It's easier to comprehend the first sentence. So, make sure you're always putting the subject of the sentence before the action of the sentence.

Naval Ravikant swears by Scott Adams' writing tips. 

But for me, it's Ravikant's ideas that make his writing truly irresistible.

According to Naval, this is why writing is such a superpower:

"Ideas are the new oil."  

Modern society requires ideas to disrupt ways of thinking. Just as oil needs to be extracted and refined to be useful, raw ideas need molding. 

And writing is a powerful tool to gain clarity and shape ideas.

That's it!

Here are 4 writing mental models for great writing by Scott Adams:

1. Simplicity wins
2. Hook them early
3. Keep sentences short
4. Subject before action

-
📌 I've been writing online since 2020.

But most people don't know that I made my first $10,000 on the internet as a ghostwriter—which ultimately led me to escape the Wall Street rat race.

Here's a 5-day crash course on how to get started: https://lnkd.in/ep3kMFAA

entries.hook: Listen:
entries.postBody:
Listen:

You become the person you want to when no one else is watching.

It's the quiet private reps, that will never be cheered, that lead to greatness.

There will always be haters, critics, and friends you lose along the way, but when you reflect on the choices you've made, there's truly only one person you need to worry about...

That's you.

Make sure you are becoming a person 8-year-old you, 80-year-old you, and you tomorrow, is proud of.

That's the only thing that matters.

entries.postBody:
Want to start writing but can't overcome the blank page?

Use these 21 templates for the next 21 days (to make writing every day for the first 3 weeks of 2024 effortless):

Before we start, a quick breakdown.

Everything you write falls into 4 buckets:

• Actionable (here's how)
• Analytical (here's what)
• Aspirational (yes, you can)
• Anthropological (here's why)

Every 1 of these 21 ideas falls into 1 of these buckets.

Let's dive in:

These are Actionable:

1. Share 1 underrated tip for solving a certain problem.

2. Share 1 framework that helps you get something done in less time or with less stress.

3. Share the 1 piece of advice to you would give to a beginner in your field.

4. Share 1 software tool that helps you the most (that most people know nothing about).

5. Share 1 helpful website (that you think is rarely used).

6. Share a step-by-step guide for accomplishing 1 specific task.

7. Share the 1 book that most helped you in learning about your topic.

8. Share the 1 podcast you listen to religiously – and what others will learn from it.

9. Share the 1 Twitter thread you've bookmarked and reread multiple times – and what you learned from it.

10. Share the 1 blog post that once you read, permanently changed the way you see the world.

Now, let's dig into Aspirational:

11. Share 1 mistake you made as a beginner – and what you learned from it.

12. Share the single most valuable thing you learned from a mentor.

13. Share your personal story – where you used to be, where you are now, and how you got here.

14. Share 1 piece of advice to your 5-years-ago self.

15. Share 1 common trait of the people you look up to.

16. Share 1 quote you live by – where you found it, and how it impacts you.

17. Share your personal playbook – how you achieved some result and the steps you took to get there.

Finally, onto Anthropological:

18. Share 1 thing you believe that everyone else doesn’t – and why you’re correct

19. Share 1 life-changing realization you made in your 20s that accelerated your success

20. Share 1 bad habit you broke in the last 12 months - and why it was holding you back

21. Share 1 common trait people who fail in your industry have—and how the reader can avoid it

Boom!

Now you have content ideas for the rest of the year and into 2024.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: The *ONLY* shortcut in life:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
The *ONLY* shortcut in life:

Learning from those ahead of you on a path you want to take.

So to accelerate your career, ask your mentors these 7 questions:

1. What is something most people think is important that I can skip entirely?

As a beginner, everything looks important.

But as you progress, you realize most things aren't.

So instead of wasting your effort in dozens of directions, ask this to figure out what to avoid.

---

2. What's something important to your daily routine you wish you started sooner?

The people ahead of you got to where they are with simple daily actions compounded over time.

So figure out what those actions are & build them into your days.

Then, stick to them for years.

--

3. What channels led to the building of your highest-quality relationships?

It's cliché, but you are the average of the 5 people you surround yourself with.

But finding the right people isn't easy—unless you know where to look.

So use this question to get some directions.

--


4. What is something you did differently than your peers 5 years ago that led to pushback, but served you in the long run?

If you do what everyone else does, you can expect to achieve what everyone else achieves (mediocrity).

Use this question to identify behaviors to shed.

--

5. What can I expect to struggle with along the way?

Growth is having problems today you would have begged to have had years ago.

So if you know which struggles are coming, you can:

• Prepare for them
• Conquer them
• Continue to grow

Which means unlocking new problems.

---

6. What is something you believed 5 years ago you had to "unlearn" to take the next step?

Progress comes from the constant cycle of:

• Identifying a bottleneck
• Removing that bottleneck

And most of the time, that bottleneck is a behavior or belief you have to "unlearn."

--

7. What's something you didn't pay enough attention to early on, then had to learn the hard way?

Early on, you're flying blind.

You don't even know what you don't know.

So the faster you can uncover these blind spots, the faster you can improve.

--

Boom—that's it.

This is your toolkit to ask high-quality questions when given the chance to talk to a mentor.

Don't waste their time "jumping on a quick call to pick their brain."


Instead, ask targeted questions they will be interested in thinking about.

Instant progress.

If you enjoyed this, I'd appreciate if you liked & reposted it so the algorithm shows it to more people ✊

Also, which one was most useful? 

Hit reply with your favorite question to let me know.

entries.hook: Over the past year, I have shared hundreds of small (but powerful) Writing Tips online.
entries.postBody:
Over the past year, I have shared hundreds of small (but powerful) Writing Tips online.

But these 10 have accumulated more than 1,000,000 views and thousands of comments & shares. 

Here they are all in one place:

1/ Create skimmable lists

• Compress what you're saying into bullets
• Make sure you're emphasizing the right points
• Then expand each point in more detail

Said differently: If you don't know the bullet points of what you're writing, you don't have clarity in what you're saying.

2/ Delete the word “that.” 

99% of the time you don’t need it.

3/ Make the last word the most important word in the sentence.

"You knew who the first President was."

This is weak because "was" is not the most important word in the sentence.

Rewrite: 

"You knew the name of the first President."

4/ Increase engagement by increasing your RoR. 

Rate of Revelation = how quickly you introduce new information to the reader. 

Low score: 1 idea stretched across hundreds of paragraphs / pages. 

High score: 10+ ideas compressed into a handful of paragraphs / pages.

5/ Prep the page 

Staring a blank page is overwhelming.

To immediately make the writing less intimidating, "Prep the page."

• Name the document
• Start w/ a working title
• Write a first sentence
• Outline your subheads

Filling in the skeleton = 10x easier.

6/ Don't use semicolons.

Most readers don't know what they mean.

(And most writers use them incorrectly.)

Commas and periods are much cleaner.

7/ Make your first sentence a short, strong declarative statement.

Ex: "There is only 1 way to get rich."

BOOM.

That short, strong declarative statement hooks the reader, and tells them what they're about to dive into.

8/ Write in different interfaces (analog & digital).

• Physical journal
• Napkins
• Looseleaf paper
• Medium UI
• Quora answer
• Reedsy book editor
• Substack newsletter
• Microsoft Word doc
• Etc.

You will see your writing differently in each UI.

9/ Cut all tiny-word-chunks

• "And so as"
• "It is for"
• "If it is"
• "Is it that"

Anytime you notice 3+ tiny words stacked next to each other, delete them.

The sentence becomes cleaner.

I call this: "Pulling the weeds between your flowers."

10/ Delete opening "i" phrases

• "I think"
• "I believe"
• "I have learned"
• "I conclude"

You're the author. 

Which means all the things you're saying are your own thoughts, beliefs, lessons learned, and conclusions reached.

Repeating so is unnecessary.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: A lot of writers misunderstand what "copywriting" actually is.
entries.postBody:
A lot of writers misunderstand what "copywriting" actually is.

Let me start by telling you what it's not:

• It's not using *shocking* words to get people's attention
• It's not making outlandish promises you can't keep
• It's not using psychology to trick customers
• It's not hard-selling customers

The whole art of copywriting is really a combination of:

1. Category Design - Moving the customer's thinking FROM somewhere... to somewhere else.

2. Languaging - Strategically use language to coin terms for the Problem you're solving, the unique Solution you're deploying, and the categorical differences between the "old" and the "new."

3. Education - Demonstrating to the potential customer that you understand their problem SO intimately, and you know how to fix it SO actionably, that they walk away from your "pitch" more educated and knowledgable regardless of whether or not they buy.

4. Packaging - Making it obvious exactly what you're offering, and how it gets done, that all the customer/client has to do is say, "Ok let's do it."

5. Pricing - Price anchoring the value of what you're offering as either a path to earning 10x more than what they pay, or save 10x more than what they pay.

If you understand how to do the above 5 things, you can sell *anything* on planet earth—without it ever feeling like a "hard sell" to the customer.

entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
The loudest critics are the least successful. 
 
Think about it: 
 
• Athletes cheer on gym newbies 
• Millionaires mentor startup founders 
• Musicians surprise karaoke singers 
 
But that guy on your LinkedIn feed? 
 
He's got an opinion on everything you do. 
 
Here's the truth: 
 
Judgment comes from the bottom. 
Support comes from those at the top. 
 
Don't let noise drown out your ambition. 
 
The world needs: 
 
• Your business idea 
• Your fitness journey 
• Your creative expression 
 
Remember: 
 
Criticism is cheap. Creation is valuable. 
 
Ignore the haters. 

Build your brand in the face of critics.

Here's your playbook: https://lnkd.in/ezvhAVk6
 
Credit: Art of Poets

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Two years is nothing.
entries.postBody:
Two years is nothing.

And also everything!

I started at 38 with zero followers and absolutely no idea what I was doing.

Two years later?

I reached 100,000 followers and achieved $1 million in revenue.

I never could have predicted that on day one.

In fact...it felt like the complete opposite was bound to happen.

The only reason I kept going was because it was fun and interesting.

Here's what most people don't understand:

You can't see the finish line at the start.
You have to trust the process & enjoy the ride.

Build when no one's buying.
Publish when no one's watching.
Keep going when everyone's quitting.

Because two years is both nothing and everything.

Nothing in the grand scheme of the work you'll do.
Everything in terms of what it can change.

Two years of consistent action?

That's 730 days of compounding.
730 chances to get better.

And at the end of those two years?

You won't recognize your life.

New income. 
New freedom. 
New options.

All because you didn't quit when it felt slow.

So if you're just starting or feeling stuck, remember this:

Two years from now, you'll wish you had started today.

Six years ago, I couldn't imagine this...

$11.4M+ business, 800K+ followers, freedom to spend my time as I please.

All because I was "dumb" enough to keep trying.

Your turn. 

Want the complete system that helped me build it?

Join 7,500+ entrepreneurs here: https://buff.ly/EFNO1Of 

(It's 75% off for early Black Friday)

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The real flex?
entries.postBody:
The real flex?

Being yourself.

Most people build for the image.

The likes.
The followers.
The validation.

They post what sounds impressive.
They share what looks good.
They hide what's real.

But here's what I learned:

Authenticity is the only competitive advantage that can't be copied.

When you're real, people trust you.
When you're honest, people connect with you.
When you're yourself, people buy from you.

I stopped trying to sound like everyone else at 38.

Started writing like I talk.
Sharing what I actually think.
Building in public with all the messy parts.

That's what grew my audience to 800K.

Not by being impressive.

By being real.

The flex isn't the image you project.

It's the courage to be yourself when it's easier to fake it.

That's the foundation of everything I teach.

The Creator MBA shows you how to turn who you actually are into a business people truly want to buy from.

111 lessons on building an authentic brand that attracts, connects, and converts.

It's how I turned my experience, expertise, and personality into an 8-figure business.

Zero fakeness required.

It's 75% off for early Black Friday: https://buff.ly/JZMm8gU

(ending soon)

Be yourself.
Build from there.

entries.hook: A clear sign of personal growth: You want everyone to win.
entries.postBody:
A clear sign of personal growth: 
 
You want everyone to win. 
 
Whenever I see people bashing others, I know I'm dealing with an immature little kid. 
 
Because the best and brightest have an infinite-game mindset. 
 
"There's a big enough pie for all of us." 
 
They root for others. 
 
Plain and simple. 
 
Here are 4 common things I see smart people doing: 
 
1. Sharing the spotlight 
2. Hosting collaborations 
3. Supporting people publicly 
4. Connecting great people together 

Here’s the “support playbook” I run: https://lnkd.in/entiSac5
 
Remember: Lift others to lift yourself. 
 
Or don't — and waste this massive opportunity. 

Consider reposting ♻️ if you think more people should be collaborating.

entries.hook: Success doesn't come from knowing it all.
entries.postBody:
Success doesn't come from knowing it all.

It's admitting you don't know it all.

Too many people fall for the comfort zone trap.
They get stuck in a loop of letting their life pass by.

Unlike what society tells us, it's not actually out of laziness.

It's because people generally hate feeling stupid.

The reality?
This can be your biggest competitive advantage. 

If you can take action when others are staying stagnant... 
You can progress significantly faster. 

Stage 1 is simply being able to admit that you don't know everything.
This alone gives you direction. 

Whenever learning something new,
I'll typically follow these steps:

1. Set crystal clear goals 🥅
↳ Understand what you want to learn and why.
↳ The "why" is the important part here. 

2. Assess your current understanding 🧠
↳ Be brutally honest here. There is no need to lie to yourself. 
↳ Then adjust your learning plan based on areas for improvement.

3. Find the highest-quality learning resources 📖
↳ Don't look in one place, expand your breadth. 
↳ Then choose educational resources that suit your learning style.

4. Create a structured learning plan 📅
↳ Develop a schedule to stick to every single day without fail.
↳ Do not miss two days in a row. That will set a new routine. 

5. Execute and stay accountable 🤝
↳ Tell someone what you're working on and be specific. 
↳ Create a small punishment with them if you don't stick with it. 

For example, when AI exploded in 2022, I said to myself:
"I will learn about this for 10 minutes a day, indefinitely."

I've stuck to it ever since.
Now my knowledge of it benefits all my businesses.

Ultimately, if you're not upskilling, you won't grow.
If you don't grow, you will not find success.

So, stay humble, keep learning, and you'll be the best version of you. 

What's something you're currently learning?
Let's all stay accountable in the comments ⬇️

♻️ Repost to help others get ahead. 
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

P.S. If you're interested in investing in your self-education this week... 

My newsletter, Step by Step, will scratch that itch:
https://lnkd.in/eXSNaDiu

entries.hook: This 15-minute trick will change your life...
entries.structural: List post, Story post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
This 15-minute trick will change your life...

The Power Down Ritual:

A Power Down Ritual is a fixed set of actions and behaviors that mentally and physically mark the end of your professional day.

I first saw the idea in a blog by Cal Newport over a decade ago, though I didn't implement one until early 2023, when I started to feel that the constant connectivity of my job was eating into my time with my wife and son.

An example of my fixed sequence might look something like this:

• Check email for any final requests requiring action.
• Check calendar and task lists for the following day.
• Do 15 minutes of prep for priority tasks of the next morning.
• Close down all applications and technology for the night.

The benefits of having a Power Down Ritual that I have observed:

1. Clear Boundaries: The ritual creates a clear set of boundaries that separate professional and personal activities. We become more present on each side of the boundary.

2. Fight Urgency: One of the biggest concerns I had with implementing this was that I would miss some urgent request that would hurt my performance. After five months of doing it, I can say that what I have actually realized is that there is very little in life that is truly urgent.

3. Enhanced Morning Productivity: By including a bit of prep for the following morning as part of my ritual, I have noticed a clear boost in my productivity on the first task of the following day. I'm ready to hit the ground running.

4. Improved Mental Health: I feel so much better in the evenings after I have had the power down. I'm more present with my wife and son, I'm not worried about the random notifications that are hitting, and I'm able to fall asleep faster.

To create your own, incorporate these three key elements:

1. Complete Final Tasks: What are the final checks that you need to perform in order to close out the tasks of the day and confirm that there is nothing remaining for you to complete? For most people, this will involve checking email and Slack with a quick scan, plus a sweep of any open projects.

2. Prepare for Tomorrow: What are the focus priorities for tomorrow? What is the first task you want to make progress on when you start work tomorrow? Do 10-15 minutes of prep work to set yourself up to hit the ground running on that priority task.

3. Initiate Power Down: Create a mental trigger for the completion of the Power Down Ritual. Cal Newport had his magic phrase ("schedule shutdown, complete"), but you can create your own less-nerdy version if you'd like.

Using those three elements, sketch out what a Power Down Ritual would look like for you.

As with everything, initial action builds momentum. Try it this week and see what happens.

Enjoy this? Share it with your network and follow me Sahil Bloom for more. 700,000+ others got this and more in their inbox last week. Join them here: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.hook: Yesterday I turned 40.
entries.postBody:
Yesterday I turned 40.

Here are 20 helpful lessons I've learned during my life.

👇🏻

1. The 2nd biggest difference between success & failure is persistence.
2. The biggest difference between success & failure is getting started.
3. 100% of people are out for themselves, even if it doesn't appear so.
4. Comparing yourself to others is the easiest way to get distracted.
5. It's difficult to build a work ethic without the right environment.
6. The most important skill to learn is how to learn on your own.
7. If you have a 10-year plan, you'll outperform most people.
8. Getting 1% better at something means leaping millions.
9. Everyone has knowledge that other people will pay for.
10. There is absolutely nothing wrong with being selfish.
11. 10% of people will hate what you do no matter what.
12. If you can't teach yourself, you'll always be a liability.
13. Don't spend time with people who don't reciprocate.
14. You won't do anything special without risk.
15. Divorce from anyone who drains you.
16. Time is the single greatest asset.
17. Money is not the root of all evil. 
18. You can't say yes to everyone.
19. Consistency trumps all traits. 
20. Nearly everyone is winging it.

While I'm bummed to leave my 30s, my wife always reminds me:

"Not everyone gets to be 40."

✌🏻

entries.hook: I got FIRED 3 times before I was 28.🤦🏼‍♂️
entries.postBody:
I got FIRED 3 times before I was 28.🤦🏼‍♂️

I NEVER hit quota in my first 4 sales jobs. 

At 28, I took a job as one of the first salespeople at a tech company in NYC. 

I'll spare you the details on how I got the job.

I moved to NYC and was paid $40k as I turned 29. 

I slept on my friend Mara's couch for 5 months. (Thanks!)

Something funny happened though: An intersection, I'll call it.

The intersection of finding a product, team, culture and city I freaking LOVED.

I got energized. Pumped. Every day. 

I worked NON-STOP. 

I won every award possible.

I got promoted 5 times. 

By 33 I became an Executive. 

I look back and cannot remember the old me. I simply can't fathom it. 

Moral of the story? Find an intersection of some thing, some people, some vibe and some place you love.

Hell, 3 out of 4 will do.

But, when they ALL intersect...well...?

Thats's when you get dangerous 🔥🔥You forget the OLD you. 

Have fun out there today. Go find the NEW you. 🤜🏼

UPDATE: This took off, and I have 1,100 unread messages in my inbox. Very unexpected. I am humbled. Wildly appreciative. Feel free to sign up to get this type of stuff regularly from me: www.justindwelsh.com

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: What if the thing that made you successful is now holding you back?
entries.postBody:
What if the thing that made you successful is now holding you back?

There's a version of "stuck" nobody talks about.

It doesn't look like failure.
It looks like something you've outgrown.

The audience keeps growing.
The revenue keeps coming.
The metrics say keep going.

But something feels off.

You show up out of obligation.
Hit publish and feel relief.
Wait for the excitement to return.

Meanwhile, something else keeps pulling at you.

A different curiosity.
A new direction.

You keep dismissing it as a distraction.

What if it's a signal?

I've followed that signal three times now.
Every pivot cost me followers.
Every pivot was exactly right.

Your business isn't a life sentence.
It's just one season.

And those seasons change.

Tomorrow's newsletter is about knowing when it's time to evolve.

I break down exactly how to navigate that shift without losing everything you've built.

Join 175,000+ entrepreneurs to get that issue in your inbox.

Plus, you'll get my free weekly tips on:

• Building visibility
• Growing an audience
• Turning expertise into income 

Get the issue + weekly tips here: https://buff.ly/nto01ey

See you on Saturday.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: This week, I bought out the lease on my BMW. And as I was signing the papers, the sales guy asked me what I do for work:
entries.postBody:
This week, I bought out the lease on my BMW. And as I was signing the papers, the sales guy asked me what I do for work:

“I’m a writer,” I said. 

“Have a couple companies. And I teach people how to write online.” 

He paused, looked at me and said, “Never heard that one before. And you’re not scared about AI taking your job?”

As he said this, he handed me a STACK of papers, that I had to sign with a pen, and initial 50 times, in order to buy my car. 

“Nope,” I said. 

Everybody thinks the future is here. 

Everyone thinks AI is going to replace everything, tomorrow. 

Meanwhile… most industries still require PEN AND PAPER. 

Yea. I’ll be fine. 

Thanks for the car.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: I can write the same idea in 4 different ways.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post, Case study / Breakdown
entries.postBody:
I can write the same idea in 4 different ways.

It's called the 4A Framework—here's how it works:

Most writers don't realize you can write every idea in 4 different ways:

• Actionable (Here's how)
• Analytical (Here's a breakdown)
• Aspirational (Yes, you can)
• Anthropological (Here's why)

Let's take an example using the topic of personal finance.

• "I Will Teach You To Be Rich" by Ramit Sethi is Actionable
• "The Changing World Order" by Ray Dalio is Analytical 
• "Rich Dad Poor Dad" by Robert Kiyosaki is Aspirational 
• "The Psychology of Money" by Morgan Housel is Anthropological 

Now, for each "A" you have different ways of writing about it:

1/ Actionable

These are actionable, implemental pieces of content.

The reader should gain some new insight or instruction they didn't have beforehand.

• Tips
• Hacks
• Resources
• Ultimate guides

Take your core idea and help the reader put it into practice.

2/ Analytical

These are breakdowns involving numbers, frameworks, and processes.

Take your core idea and support it with numbers and analysis.

• Trends
• Reasons
• Numbers
• Examples
• Swipe files
• Teardowns

Help the reader unlock a new way of thinking.

3/ Aspirational

These are stories of how you or others put your core idea into practice.

• Traits
• Stories
• Quotes
• Lessons
• Mistakes
• Reflections

Help the reader understand the benefits they unlock when they see the world through this new lens.

4/ Anthropological

These are things that speak to universal human nature.

• Fears
• Failures
• Struggles
• Limiting beliefs
• Misconceptions

Create a sense of urgency for the reader to fully embrace your core idea or be forever left behind.

That's it!

If you use this framework, you'll never be short of writing ideas again.

---
📌 Want more writing tips?

Here’s a FREE 5-day email course with everything you need to:

• Find your niche
• Generate hundreds of ideas
• Start going viral with ease

Get instant access here: https://lnkd.in/gHVMYgYi

entries.postBody:
The myth of overnight success:

It doesn't exist.

What looks like luck is usually:

• Years of unseen effort
• Relentless persistence
• Countless failures and lessons

Most people:

• See the highlight reel
• Chalk success up to "luck"
• Ignore the behind-the-scenes work

Success is an iceberg.

The visible part is tiny compared to what's beneath.

Stop waiting for your lucky break and start creating it through your work.

Your "big break" is built daily, not discovered.

Want to create your own luck?

There's no better place than LinkedIn.

Here's your playbook: https://lnkd.in/eh9pVVuf

entries.postBody:
Winning requires reps.

There's only 1 way to compensate for lack of skill:

Volume.

Talent is a bit of a myth.

Everything you are now good at, you were once bad at.

The difference is, you did it.

You went to bat.

Over and over.

Until you hit the home run.

Put in the reps at smaller weights → you'll be ready lift the big ones when the time comes.

↓

You follow 100 accounts, only 1 changes your business and your life. Could this be the one? 😉 → follow Codie A. Sanchez

entries.creator: Lara Acosta
entries.hook: If my LinkedIn went to zero today...
entries.postBody:
If my LinkedIn went to zero today...

Here's exactly how I would build my business back:

(Save + Repost this ♻️ for later)

 1.⁠ ⁠I'd identify what I call a 'fast advantage'. 

•⁠  ⁠A skillset that makes me stand out (writing)
•⁠  ⁠An experience that makes me relatable (failing)
•⁠  ⁠Social proof I could leverage (Forbes features)

This is how I'd go from 0 followers to 10k in a month.

 2.⁠ ⁠I'd create highly actionable content to get clients.

•⁠  ⁠Find the main pain points 1 person has.
•⁠  ⁠Create content on how I'd solve them.
•⁠  ⁠Repeat until I get my first lead. 

I.e: How to grow an email list using LinkedIn. 
I.e: How to tell viral stories on LinkedIn in 3 steps. 

 3.⁠ ⁠I'd have 2 main offers ready from day 1 to cash in fast. 

•⁠  ⁠A done-for-you offer ($3k+ service)
•⁠  ⁠A do-it-yourself offer (A $99 digital product)

Fastest way to win clients through content.

 4.⁠ ⁠I'd split content 50/50 stories and education. 

Stories will make me relatable to a broad audience.
Education will make me interesting to a niche audience. 

Using both, I'd build a profitable personal brand. 

 5.⁠ ⁠I'd write broad hooks but narrow and niche content.

Broad hook: "Here's a career hack that saved me"
Narrow re-hook: "After beating burnout at 27"
Niche content: "Start writing on LinkedIn"

Hits the spot for multiple audiences maximing my reach. 
I get to be in front of 10x the people with the same effort.

 6.⁠ ⁠I'd use social proof in 90% of my posts. 
 7.⁠ ⁠I'd use validated keywords in every hook. 

I.e: I worked with 100+ founders this year.
Here's how I taught them how to use AI to write.

→ "Worked with 100+ founders" is the social proof
→ "How to" + AI to write" are the right keywords

(I'll drop a list of them in the comments too). 

 8.⁠ ⁠I'd launch weekly lead magnets. 

•⁠  ⁠Solving a specific problem.
•⁠  ⁠Getting them to join my newsletter to get it.
•⁠  ⁠Emailing people who downloaded it with an offer. 

I.e: Breaking down Justin welsh's $1M funnel.

Helps gain followers and leads at the same time.
Win-win.

 9.⁠ ⁠I'd work for free for 3x people with massive personal brands on LinkedIn and use them as case studies. 

This is a career accelerator (if you do good work). 

10.⁠ ⁠I'd be in the DMs for 2 hours a day. 

•⁠  ⁠I'd message those who like and comment on my post.
•⁠  ⁠I'd message those who look at my profile. 

Wouldn't pitch, just ask the right leading questions and see if they are on the market for my service. 

That's literally it. 10 simple steps to making $$$ on LI. 

Fame is good, cash is great but both combined... 

Make you unstoppable. Trust me, I did it. 

Enrollment for my new programme is now open: https://lnkd.in/e2Br4TTK

entries.hook: Stay locked in.
entries.postBody:
Stay locked in.

No time for drama.
No time for gossip.
No time for comparisons.

Just you and your progress.

Most people waste energy on things that don't matter.

Scrolling.
Complaining.
Watching what everyone else is doing.

But the people who win?

They're too busy building.

I learned this the hard way.

When I was stuck, I was distracted.
When I was growing, I was locked in.

The difference wasn't talent.

It was focus.

Discipline isn't about doing more.

It's about eliminating everything that doesn't move you forward.

Your growth demands your full attention.

Everything else is noise.

Every Saturday morning, I send one focused email to 175,000+ entrepreneurs.

No fluff. No filler. Just one actionable idea to help you build.

It takes 4 minutes to read.

It's free.

And it's designed to keep you locked in while everyone else gets distracted.

Join here: https://buff.ly/VzhEczR

Stay focused.

Keep building.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Working for yourself is really hard.
entries.postBody:
Working for yourself is really hard.

It often involves:

1. Wrestling with self-doubt, questioning if you're cut out for this.

2. Battling impostor syndrome, feeling like you don’t belong.

3. Juggling multiple responsibilities, trying to keep all balls in the air.

4. Navigating financial challenges, which can be scary as hell.

Yes, it's hard. 

But truth be told, every path is hard.

So, as we roll toward 2024, give yourself some time to think:

Are the challenges you're working on in your current career fulfilling?

If the answer is no, it's never too late to build something new. Something small that may, someday, be something big. 

The beginning of a new year is the perfect time to embrace change and choose the 'right hard' for your journey.

Are you?

~~~

If you found this helpful, consider resharing ♻️ and follow me Justin Welsh for more content like this.

entries.creator: Chris Donnelly
entries.hook: Great leaders know what to look for when hiring.
entries.postBody:
Great leaders know what to look for when hiring.

It might not be what you think:

While experience is definitely useful at the start...
The best leaders understand that's just one aspect of hiring.

There are other to consider, such as:

- The way they interact with others (i.e. introvert vs extrovert).
- Soft skills like communication, negotiation, EQ, etc.
- Have they built something outside of work?
- Their level of ambition & hunger.

If you hire for experience, you will have a technically skilled team.
But perhaps not one that is aligned with your mission and purpose.

That's more important than people realise.

Instead, look for candidates with these traits:

🧠 Someone with a growth mindset who will learn fast.
🤝 Someone that is incredibly easy to work with.
☄️ Someone who will energise others to achieve their goals.
💡 Someone with the ability to think outside the box.
📖 Someone who is constantly looking to upskill themselves.

But recognising a person with these traits immediately is difficult.

However, I have discovered some tricks for uncovering it.
I ask these 3 questions to find out if they're the right fit:

1. Ask them to tell a story.
↳ Storytelling is a key skill for any role.
↳ This also helps you get a feel for their personality.

2. Ask them what they like to do outside work.
↳ If it aligns with the role, that's a great sign.
↳ Evidence of a hobby is a great sign of their passion.

3. Ask what their weaknesses are.
↳ If they give a generic answer, that's a red flag.
↳ Someone who's willing to admit their flaws will grow.

These should help you get a gauge for who is right for you.

Ultimately, experience is just the tip of the iceberg.
The full picture is much deeper.

What else should a leader look for when hiring?
Let me know in the comments ⬇️

♻️ Repost this if you agree.
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

For Free On Thursday at 5.30pm GMT

Sign up here: 
https://lnkd.in/eMYw-JvF

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The people winning aren't smarter.
entries.postBody:
The people winning aren't smarter.

They just never stopped learning.

I see this pattern everywhere.

The person who thinks they know it all?

Stuck at the same level for years.

The person who's constantly curious?

Rising fast.

Here's why:

The world changes every day.

What worked last year doesn't work now.
What you learned in school is already outdated.
What got you here won't get you there.

So you've got two choices:

Keep learning.
Or get left behind.

I'm 44 and I still spend hours every week learning.

Reading books.
Testing new strategies.
Staying curious.

Not because I have to.

Because that's what keeps me relevant.

The moment you think you've figured it all out is the moment you start falling behind.

Your competition isn't standing still.
The market isn't standing still.
Technology isn't standing still.

So why would you?

Stay sharp.
Stay curious.
Stay growing.

That's how you keep rising.

If you want to join over 175,000 online entrepreneurs lifting their audience and business-building skills each week, consider joining my newsletter, The Saturday Solopreneur.

I send out one tip to help you grow your audience and revenue every Saturday.

4 minutes or less guaranteed.

Join free here: https://buff.ly/3ZzyLhl

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: 19 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:
entries.postBody:
19 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

2. Successful people are neither smarter nor more creative than you. 

They are simply executing better.

3. Success comes from doing somewhat-monotonous things every day for years – but no one wants to hear that.

4. You can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation.

5. 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity.

6. You said you would start months ago.

7. You can complain about the game or you can learn the rules.

8. The market rewards not the best product, but the product who best positions themselves as the obvious solution.

9. If you did everything you said you'd do, your results would be 10x better than they are. 

10. 99% of people would rather be uncomfortable and unhappy than uncertain.

11. No one on Earth thinks about you as much as you think they do.

12. You know exactly what you should be doing, so stop chasing shiny objects. 

Instead, figure out why you aren't doing what you know you should be. 

13. If you spent half as many hours taking action as you did thinking about taking action, you would be in a better spot. 

14. If you are reading productivity books, you are working on the wrong project.

15. You can't deposit the number of books you've read or followers you have in a bank account.

16. You have three finite resources: time, energy, and attention.

Every time you waste them, they're gone forever.

17. The people you look up to are not that special and are no more talented than you are.

18. If you're afraid to publish things online, you have an ego problem.

19. Being "busy" is a sign you lack leverage and are delegating poorly.
---
📌 If you enjoyed this, you'll love my newsletter.

In it, I share no-fluff frameworks for making personal progress: dickiesdigest.com

entries.hook: Here's how to simplify your pitch and 10x your sales:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
Here's how to simplify your pitch and 10x your sales:

1. Talk less, sell more.

Short sentences = more sales.

Hemingway once bet he could write a story in 6 words that'd make you feel something: "For sale: baby shoes, never worn."

Your pitch should pack the same punch.

2. Complexity is for people who want to feel smart, not be effective.

[...continues with 13 numbered tactics covering: short sentences, complexity = ego, read your pitch out loud, "Be good, be seen, be gone", speak like your customer, every word earns its place, abstract vs concrete (❌/✅ examples), emotion + logic, Grandma Test, Benefits > Features > Dreams, power words (You/Free/Because/Instantly/New)...]

Two final things to keep in mind...

Simplicity isn't just for sales.

Apply these principles to:
- your business operations
- your thinking processes
- your next investment
- your relationships
- your to do list

Sales isn't just for car dealerships.

You pitch when you:
- Negotiate a raise
- Interview for a job
- Post on social media
- Hire someone for a job
- Talk to an owner about buying their biz

PS: Have a complex pitch deck? Try distilling it to a Tear Sheet. Use this template → https://lnkd.in/gxA3_n2M

entries.postBody:
6 months, and we finished the The LinkedIn GPT OS.  

It saves 100+ hours and you can have it for FREE ↓

Comment "GPTOS" and I will personally send it to you.

Most founders are drowning in content demands whilst their business suffers.

They turn to AI for help, but the outputs are rubbish...
Because generic tools with no strategy only result in low-quality outputs. 

That's why team and I have spent HOURS custom-building our own.

Now, we have an all-in-one LinkedIn GPT OS that saves us hours of time... 

Whilst maintaining a supremely high content quality.

entries.hook: I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight. 

No fancy journal. No complex rules. 

Just a pen, paper, and 4 simple prompts to help focus my mind and find clarity.

Here's the dead-simple system I use every day:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

---
Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

---
Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

---
Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

---
I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 10 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

---
📌 Looking to start writing online?

Here’s a FREE 13,000-word Ultimate Guide + email course with everything you need to:

• Build a daily writing habit
• Generate hundreds of ideas
• Start going viral with ease

Get it here: https://lnkd.in/eBSCxsqR

entries.hook: I wasted years as a horrible writer.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
I wasted years as a horrible writer.

But then I found Tim Ferriss's writing routine and the mental models behind his 5 New York Times best-selling books.

4 mental models you can start using today:

1. Two "crappy pages" per day

Tim sets a low bar to build a consistent daily writing habit.

While writing, Tim thinks, writes, and edits as if he is catching up with an old friend over a few drinks.

This immediately takes the pressure off producing "quality."

2. Voice notes and interviews

When Tim is stuck with a chapter, he hires someone to interview him about the topic.

This thinking out loud forces him to test and refine his core ideas.

3. Fear Setting Exercise

Self-doubt haunted Tim, so he turned to the Stoic philosophy of Seneca, who taught:

“We suffer more in imagination than in reality.”

Tim created a practice to see that he could handle whatever "bad" situation might arise. This lowered the stakes and helped him to start writing.

4. Copywork

To find his voice, Tim copied the styles of journalists he admired, like Chip Heath and Malcolm Gladwell.

• He'd find a piece he admired
• Then rewrite word-by-word digitally or by hand

Stephen King is also a big believer in this practice, too.

One final note:

Tim pitched "The 4-Hour Workweek" to 27 publishers. 

26 rejected him.

But his lucky break came when he met Jack Canfield, author of "Chicken Soup for the Soul."

Jack saw the book's potential and pulled strings to get it sold.

To date, Tim has sold over 2.1 million copies of the book.

The lesson?

Keep trying and putting in the work—you create your own luck.

That's it!

📌 I study best-selling writers to improve my craft.

But the fastest way to monetize as a writer is to become a Premium Ghostwriter.

It's how I built a $180k/month agency in less than 18 months.

Become a Premium Ghostwriter in 5 steps here (it's free): https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Observation / Contrarian take
entries.postBody:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting.

And before they know it, years have gone by and it's too late to change.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

The goal of the monthly review:

Block 60 minutes to slowly process the month.

It's easy to get caught up in the day to day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

And after trying 1000+ reflection questions, I chose each question for a specific reason.

Let's start with the first:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things that you will look back on with pride & fond memories at the end of the year.

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, our focus is on the negative. But this questions helps us celebrate all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

From there, you ask a more important question:

---
2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—because something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

And by constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By constantly iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

---
3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page in 2 columns and brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

The goal here is to identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

---
4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive *and* negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to *double down* on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to *stop doing entirely* that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

---
5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a quick list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

---
And just like that, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things throughout the month.

• Wins & milestones
• Realizations & decisions
• Things going well & things to improve

Then, at the end of the year, you collect these monthly reviews and distill the entire year.

entries.creator: Lara Acosta
entries.hook: You don't need algorithm hacks to grow on LinkedIn.
entries.postBody:
You don't need algorithm hacks to grow on LinkedIn.

I spent the last 90 days testing every growth hack I could find. Here's what actually works:

(Based on analyzing content in different niches)

1. Hyper-specific content (3x a week)

• Solve one exact problem per post
• Use industry language your clients use
• Speak directly to one person

Adam Robinson has mastered this for B2B. 
Nausheen I. Chen is killing it for B2C. 

Address their problems directly. 

i.e "Stop losing credibility with your body language."

2. Proof-First Content

80% tactical step-by-step frameworks and screenshots.
20% stories on how you're doing it (use stories)

The 80% builds trust, the 20% engages them more. 

Educate and storytell. Always both. 

3. LinkedIn Video (let me explain...)

Video reach might be down right now.
But it's the fastest way to prove authority. 

LinkedIn video is ok.
Linkedin Lives? They're a goldmine.

We had a LinkedIn live with 200 people.
Made $10,000 from it. 

People buy from people they trust. Video accelerates this! 

I'd limit these to 2 a month, though, no pressure. 

I left a checklist for you in the image below for you to script your first Linkedin video too btw.

This is the no-bs blueprint for entrepreneurs. 

It's not about more volume, it's about more insights.

Dominating LinkedIn isn't just about virality right now.
It's about building a brand that earns trust.

Keep your focus on your 1 thing. 

The rest will follow.

I've just recorded a private video on how to dominate "storytelling" on LinkedIn like an expert. 

If you want it ♻️ repost this post and I'll send it to you (20 people max). 

PS: Which step do you struggle with the most? 

--
PS: Got a surprise for you today, get 50% off my programmes until the end of November here: https://lnkd.in/e4RKsGx9

entries.hook: Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office?
entries.postBody:
Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office? 
 
Or spend all day at your desk? 
 
Me too. 
 
Sure...I understand the camaraderie. 
 
And some things are more easily done in person than virtually. I get that. 
 
But here's something else I think: 
 
Success is not about long hours in the office. Or sitting at your desk from 9 to 5. 
 
It's about good ideas and sharp execution. 
 
And sometimes, the best ideas come when you're away from your desk. 
 
Like at the dinner table, on a run, or playing with your kids. 
 
Spending time with family isn't just nice, it helps us think better. 
 
Don't get stuck on the old idea that more hours at work means more success. 
 
Try something different. 
 
Spend more time living life, and you might find you're better at your job. 
 
Take a step back and think about this. 
 
Maybe a new way to measure success is in the quality of our ideas, and how we execute on them - not the quantity of our hours. 
 
Your desk isn't going anywhere, but your life is. 
 
Build life-work balance. Not the other way around.

Here’s how: https://lnkd.in/ewg6y-i5
 
Repost ♻️ if you think being forced back to the office & desk is silly.

Build your brand in the face of critics.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: I noticed something funny about decision-making.
entries.postBody:
I noticed something funny about decision-making.

When I'm agitated?

Every option seems important and urgent. 

I quickly jump between choices, convinced each one could be the best or worst decision of my life.

But when I deliberately slow down — take a walk, sit quietly, sleep on it — the noise fades.

The complicated becomes simple. 
The options reduce to two or three. 
The unimportant falls away.

The calmer I am, the more clearly I can see what matters and what doesn't.

This works for everything: business decisions, creative problems, and even relationship issues.

So, next time you're stuck or overwhelmed, try this experiment: 

Wait. 

Do nothing for a day. 
Let your mind settle.
Then decide.

Don't be fooled by the feeling that urgency equals importance. 

Often, it's the opposite.

The best decisions come from a quiet mind.

P.S. If you enjoyed this writing, learn how I built an audience of 700K+ writing for 84.7M+ impressions monthly here: https://lnkd.in/eh9pVVuf

Image credit: pathsofstoicism
