Nicolas Cole 🚢👻 reposted this

At 24, a stranger on the internet paid me $5,000.

The crazy part?

I thought they'd sent it by mistake!

But I'd actually just stumbled across an insanely lucrative business anyone can start:

Ghostwriting.

Everyone should have writing clients who pay $5,000+ for a project.

(This is ultimately how I quit my job on Wall Street so I could travel and work from anywhere in the world.)

But most people are stuck figuring out:

• What service to sell
• Which clients to target
• How to charge premium rates

So, last month, Nicolas Cole 🚢👻 and I put together the Writer Positioning Playbook to help you:

• Escape the freelancer rat race
• Confidently charge high-ticket prices
• Land ideal clients who value your expertise

Inside, you'll find:

• The 1 simple shift to 10x your rates overnight
• A 3-step framework to upgrade your Positioning
• An AI prompt to find 5 high-paying clients for YOUR specific background

Plus, you'll see real examples from a $3M ghostwriting agency.

Want access?

Comment "playbook" and I'll send it across (for free).

And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "playbook" and I'll DM it to you.

entries.hook: 5 uncomfortable truths about success:
entries.postBody:
5 uncomfortable truths about success:

1. You can't be a real leader and a people pleaser.
2. One day they'll go from judging you to bragging how they know you.
3. You win in life by being ok with people: not liking you, disagreeing with you, and wishing you'd fail.
4. It's supposed to be hard, and you're supposed to do it anyway.
5. The underrated part about being rich... you get to do what you want, say what you want, and people unfollowing or not agreeing matters ZERO.

Hope this one puts a little air in your tires today 😉

Btw - feel free to follow me Codie A. Sanchez if you haven't yet

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: This is one of my favorite speeches of all time…
entries.postBody:
This is one of my favorite speeches of all time…

It always hits me hard:

“You get up every day—you’re entitled to nothing. Nobody owes you nothing. You could have talent, but if you don’t have discipline, and you don’t execute, you don’t focus… What do you get? Nothing. If you’re complacent and not paying attention to detail… What does that get you? Nothing. So nothing is acceptable but your best.”

This is a harsh reality of life:

Talent is meaningless without discipline.
Ambition is worthless without execution.
Focus is the difference between potential and reality.

You’re not entitled to success—it’s earned in the quiet, thankless moments when no one is watching.

The antidote to entitlement is relentless ownership.

Show up fully. Pay attention to the details. Do your best—every single day.

Because in a world where everyone wants the reward, it’s those who refuse to accept anything less than their best who build something worth being proud of.

If this resonated, repost to your network ♻️ and follow Sahil Bloom for more.

Want my best thinking on self-improvement and growth? Try The Curiosity Chronicle, my free newsletter. Join 800,000+ here: https://lnkd.in/esGsF85Q

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: I got married this past week in Cartagena, Colombia!
entries.postBody:
I got married this past week in Cartagena, Colombia!

Here's something I wrote for my wife, and shared during our ceremony, about what it means to find your life partner:

~
One of my favorite writers, Charles Bukowski,
said the problem is,
we look for someone to grow old together,
When the secret is to find someone to stay a child with.

When I was a child,
I asked my mother for her definition of love.
She said love is the ability to grow together.
That life is hard, people will change,
But your love for each other should always remain.

I am not the same person I was when we met.
And in the best way, neither are you.
I had never been on a safari in South Africa.
You had never been to a Chicago Blackhawks hockey game.
I hadn’t lived anywhere other than the Midwest.
And you had lived all over the world, but weren’t sure which place to call Home.

In the beginning of our relationship we used to say,
“Home isn’t a place, it’s a feeling.”
Because we lived a thousand miles apart, and getting to know each other wasn’t convenient.
Being together meant choosing each other.
Getting on planes. Meeting up in different cities.
And sad goodbyes in airports,
Until the next time we’d see each other,
And finally feel at home again.

We’re getting married today, but we committed to each other a long time ago.
From the day we met, it felt like everything else in our life was negotiable—except for our relationship.
We could live anywhere.
We could do anything.
As long as we were together.

I know, and you know, that I wouldn’t be the man I am today without you.
You are my mirror,
My greatest motivator, my battery pack, my passport to the world.
You have taught me more about myself than I could have ever learned on my own.
You have made me stronger.
You have made me softer.
But most of all, you have taught me what it means to take care of someone.
To be selfless. To love without conditions.
And to meet each other halfway,
And not just love the things we share in common,
But love what makes us different.

So if love is the ability to grow together,
And the secret is to find someone to stay a child with,
Then I know you are my partner for life.
Because years have passed,
We’ve grown and changed,
I am not the same person I was when we met,
And in the best way, neither are you.
But I still feel like we’re kids
and our love has always remained.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Time is ruthlessly honest.
entries.postBody:
Time is ruthlessly honest.

It reveals how today's choices shape your future.

Most people work hard their entire lives...

• Commuting early
• Fighting for promotions
• Striving for performance
• Positioning themselves for a raise
• Trying desperately to climb the ladder

None of these things are bad.

I spent 16 years doing the same.

But there IS an alternative.

It's extremely hard.
It takes a really long time.
It requires a massive commitment.
A lot of people who try will not be successful.

But if you're going to try, it all starts with an investment in yourself.

A year from now, you'll wish you had:

• Taken that risk
• Built that audience
• Started that business

The gap between dreaming and doing?

It's called today.

Don't wait for permission.
Stop dreaming about freedom.

Take step one.

This time next year, you'll thank yourself.

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: Life "cheat codes" everyone should know:
entries.postBody:
Life "cheat codes" everyone should know:

1. When you think something nice about someone, let them know right then.

2. Never think twice about investments in yourself. They pay dividends for a long time.

3. Put your alarm clock in the bathroom. If you have to get up to turn it off, you won't snooze.

4. If you want to get better at anything, do it for 30 minutes per day for 30 straight days.

5. Hire a writer to document your parents' childhood stories. Print it with old photos.

6. Write down three things you’re grateful for before bed. Read them out loud when you wake up.

7. Go for a 15 minute walk every morning. It has a positive impact on your mood, sleep, metabolism, digestion, and more.

8. Always pursue the path that has the larger luck surface area.

9. Tell your partner one thing you appreciate about them every single day.

10. Do a few things that you'll be excited to tell your kids about someday. Create stories worth telling.

11. Spend 15 minutes every evening preparing for your first focus tasks the next morning.

12. If someone tries to put down your accomplishments, cut them out of your life.

13. Take yourself out for a meal alone once each month. It’s insanely freeing—a meditative experience.

14. When you're starting your career, swallow the frog for your boss to get ahead. Observe your boss, figure out what they hate doing, and take it off their plate. Easy win.

15. When someone is going through hell, just say "I’m with you." Advice is minimally impactful. The notion that someone is with you is 10x more powerful.

16. If someone regularly brags about their wealth, income, or success, just assume the reality is about 50% of what they say.

17. Reread your favorite books annually. You may read thousands of books in your life, but there will only be a few that deeply change you.

18. Never delay difficult conversations. If you're nervous, do it on a walk, it makes it much easier.

19. Do the "old fashioned" things well. Look people in the eye, have a firm handshake, and always stay true to your word.

20. If you're about to say yes to something on the assumption that you'll have more time for it in the future, say no instead.

21. Give people a second chance, but never a third.

22. If you want something—and have put in the work to deserve it—ask for it.

23. Never take advice from people on the sidelines.

24. Learn one dance move that you can reliably bust out when you inevitably get pushed into the center of a dance circle at a wedding or event.

25. Be bored for at least 15 minutes per day. Boredom unlocks creativity.

26. Invest in personalized stationery and use it regularly.

27. Spend more time doing things you never regret.

28. Make decisions that your 80-year-old self and 10-year-old self would be proud of.

29. Do one hard thing every day when you could do it the easy way.

30. Be interested in everyone you encounter.

***

What “cheat codes” would you add to the list?

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: A legendary writer you've never heard of:
entries.postBody:
A legendary writer you've never heard of:

Henry Miller.

He wrote books so good (and so controversial) the US banned them from publication for 30 years.

In 1930, Henry made a list of 11 Commandments that are a must-read for any aspiring creative:

1. Work on one thing at a time until finished

Henry's biggest superpower: focus. 

If you give something 100% of your attention (versus giving it 90%) - it's not 10% better, it's 10 times better.

2. Start no more new books, add no more new material to "Black Spring."

At the time of writing these rules, Henry was working on his book "Black Spring."

So this was a two-fold reminder:

• Stop chasing shiny objects
• Edit your work with subtraction, not addition

3. Don't be nervous. Work calmly, joyously, recklessly, on what is in hand.

Calmly, joyously, recklessly - it's worth rereading those words.

You can *feel* what it's like to work in a state like that just from reading them.

4. Work according to Program and not according to mood. Stop at the appointed time!

The crux of Henry's Program was two-fold:

• Consistency
• Constraint

He wrote every day - but not all day. 

5. When you can't create, then you can work.

To Henry, "creating" and "working" were two separate things.

Today, these are the meetings, tasks, emails, and other things that gnaw at our precious attention.

Henry did those things when he could no longer create.

6. Cement a little every day, rather than add new fertilizers. 

"Cementing" means to finish something. 

"Fertilizing" is planning, "researching" and preparing, which are really procrastinating.

More cement, less fertilizer.

7. Keep human! See people, go places, drink if you feel like it.

As much as Henry enjoyed the creative writing process, he knew one thing:

The best writing is not a result. It's a byproduct of a life well-lived.

8. Don't be a draught-horse! Work with pleasure only.

Henry had to remind himself: 

Work every day but enjoy the journey as well.

9. Discard the Program when you feel like it—but go back to it the next day. Concentrate. Narrow. Exclude.

A man way before his time, Henry pioneered the "2 Day Rule."

On the rare days where he could not get it going, he would give it up.

But he made sure to never miss twice.

10. Forget the books you want to write. Think only of the book you are writing.

As if he forgot the very first commandment, Henry reminds himself to stop chasing shiny new objects and focus on the book at hand.

There's a lesson here that he had to repeat this rule to himself.

11. Write first and always. Painting, music, friends, cinema, all these come afterward.

And to round it out, Henry finishes with the foundation of his Program:

Write first and always.

-

📌 And if you want more writing tips, here's a free 13,000-word Ultimate Guide on how to start writing online. 

Join over 150,000 people who finally started writing after reading it: https://lnkd.in/eeNFKWP7

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: I recently got asked about career advice I wish I had received when I was starting out.
entries.postBody:
I recently got asked about career advice I wish I had received when I was starting out.

Here are 8 pieces of advice I know at 32 I wish I knew at 22:

1. Build a reputation for reliability.

Reliability is one of the most important traits in your career. You can get pretty damn far by just being someone that people can count on to show up and do the work. Being reliable is entirely free and doesn't require any talent or luck.

2. Be the person who just figures it out.

Early on, you'll be given a lot of tasks you have no idea how to complete. There's nothing more valuable than someone who can just figure it out. Do some work, ask the key questions, get it done. People will fight over you.

3. Swallow the Frog for your boss.

This is one of the greatest "hacks" to get ahead early in your career. Observe your boss, figure out what they hate doing, learn to do it, and take it off their plate. Easy win.

4. Build storytelling skills.

World-changing CEOs aren't the smartest or most talented in their organizations. They are exceptional at: (1) Aggregating data and (2) Communicating it simply & effectively. Data in, story out. Build that skill and you'll always be valuable.

5. Work hard first (and smart later).

It's in vogue to say that working smart is all that matters. Wrong. If you want to accomplish anything significant, you have to work hard. Build a reputation for hard work—take pride in it. Then you can start to build leverage to work smart.

6. Do the "old fashioned" things well.

There are simple things that still stand out. Look people in the eye, do what you say you'll do, be early, practice good posture, have a confident handshake. It sounds silly, but these things are all free and will never go out of style.

7. Show up early, stay late.

Showing up early and staying late is a free way to materially increase your luck surface area. The most interesting side conversations come up before meetings start or after they end. When you're in the room, you're more likely to get pulled into a follow-up call, coffee, or discussion. Being in the room pays off handsomely in the long run.

8. Dive through cracked doors.

I recently had an experience that brings this advice to life: These two young guys were trying to work with me on a project but my schedule was tough for a call. They said they had to be in NY for a meeting the next day and offered to meet in person. I said ok. We met, hit it off, and are working together. They later told me they didn’t need to be in NY at all and booked flights after I said yes. I'd always bet on people with that kind of energy. If someone cracks open a door that may present an opportunity, dive through it.

Embrace those 8 pieces of advice and you'll stand out and be on the right track.

If you enjoyed this or learned something, follow me Sahil Bloom for more in the future!

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: 7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":
entries.structural: List post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":

When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.

8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here's what I learned:

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟏: 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐝𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐞.

I wrote every day in my studio. Even in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers to cool off.

Only difference today? AC.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟐: 𝐀𝐥𝐥 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐧𝐞𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐬 𝐚 𝐛𝐞𝐝 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚 𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐤.

For the first 3 years after college, I slept on a $79 air mattress. My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

Today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed & desk.

It's all you need.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟑: 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐨𝐟 𝐬𝐮𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of internet for 4 years.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟒: 𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐟𝐢𝐱 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed of how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities were my own and money wouldn't fix them.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟓: 𝐈𝐟 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐜𝐚𝐧'𝐭 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞, 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐰𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll do Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't convenient.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟔: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐯𝐞, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐲 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐝.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago:

• TV
• Travel
• Disposable income

In my 20s, I didn't have these options.

This was a gift.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟕: "𝐖𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠" 𝐢𝐬 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐟𝐮𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 "𝐇𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠".

Two days after I moved into my dream condo, I had my weekly therapy session.

"Now what?" I asked.

I'd spent a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

I got "here" and didn't know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I ghostwrote for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home: a ranch with a home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different. You have so many options!"

He shook his head, "Actually, it's killing me."

He planned to rent a city studio apt to make his next album.

We think the journey will get easier once we've achieved our dreams or made a certain amount of money.

It's the opposite.

It gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Everything might change.

But the work won't.

-
📌 Ghostwriting completely changed my life.

Here's how to land $5k-$10k clients & get paid to learn & build your network:

https://lnkd.in/eGAyHceM

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Experience is brutal.
entries.postBody:
Experience is brutal.

It gives you the test first.
Then the lesson.

You launch and fail.
Then you learn why.

You post and get ignored.
Then you learn what works.

You build and hear crickets.
Then you learn what people actually want.

There's no shortcut around this.

Nothing replaces experience.
No mentor can do it for you.
No amount of planning prevents it.

You have to DO it.
You have to LIVE it.

I failed for years before I figured out what worked.

Lost money on bad ideas.
Built things nobody wanted.
Wasted time on the wrong strategies.

But every failure taught me something I couldn't learn any other way.

That's how you get good.

Not by avoiding mistakes.
By making them faster and learning from them.

The test comes first.
The lesson comes after.
That's the deal.

I finally recorded 111 (hard-earned) lessons on building an audience and business from scratch, so that you don't have to go through as many of them.

- Choosing a profitable idea
- Separating yourself from the pack
- Using content marketing to guide people
- Funnel building + conversion optimization
- Scaling from 5 to 6 to 7-figures over the years

75% off today for early Black Friday.

Start watching here: https://buff.ly/JZMm8gU

entries.hook: Society's definition of wealth is expensive.
entries.postBody:
Society's definition of wealth is expensive.

It'll cost you everything.

The price of being 100% focused on "looking successful" is:

• Your time
• Your health
• Your relationships
• Your peace of mind

The wealthiest people I know?

• Drive normal cars
• Live in normal homes
• Keep very low overhead

Their real assets stack up like this:

1. Time freedom
2. Mental clarity
3. Physical health
4. Ability to say "no"

Always chasing the appearance of wealth keeps you from building *actual* wealth.

Want to build wealth that really matters?

I've written 230+ free guides to building a lean, profitable, one-person business online.

Give them a read: https://lnkd.in/eayQMteS

True wealth is having enough without needing more.

entries.postBody:
Most people don't fail at business building.

They just never start.

Why?

Because it feels safer to:

• Stay in the job they hate
• Dream about "someday"
• Watch others take action
• Discuss deas that never launch

But here's the truth about work:

Playing it safe is the riskiest move you can make.

The people who succeed aren't special.
They just stopped avoiding failure.

Get. Out. Of. Your. Comfort. Zone.

Read this, go write something about your knowledge or expertise and publish it.

Who cares if it sucks?

The day you start is the day you start getting better.

If you really don't know how to start, I put together the most comprehensive research on LinkedIn marketing and packaged it in 90 min. of video: https://buff.ly/yOV2A1G

Watch it.

Then go take action TODAY.

When you're done, come tell me about it in the comments.

entries.hook: Most people wait for permission.
entries.structural: Question post, How-to / Framework post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
Most people wait for permission.

For someone to believe in them.
For the perfect moment.
For the stars to align.

I waited 38 years.

Then I realized:

Nobody's coming to save me.
Nobody's going to discover me.
Nobody's going to hand me success.

It's going to happen because I'm going to make it happen.

That shift changed everything.

From hoping to deciding.
From wishing to building.
From waiting to creating.

780K followers didn't find me.
I built an audience.

$10M didn't appear.
I created value.

Freedom didn't arrive.
I took it.

Every morning you choose:

Wait for it or make it.

The people you admire?

They got tired of hoping.
They started building.

Stop asking "What if?"
Start saying "I will."

Your dreams aren't going to chase themselves.

Come join us in creating the future you want by learning how to put yourself out there.

By showing people why YOU are worth paying attention to.

I'll show you the same daily playbook that over 45,000 students and hundreds of LinkedIn Top Voices have used to build brands that matter: https://buff.ly/wJvIV7r

That people care about and listen to.

Watch it.

Then stop waiting and start making it happen.

entries.hook: Over the last 5 years, I lost 100 pounds and wrote every morning for 90 minutes.
entries.postBody:
Over the last 5 years, I lost 100 pounds and wrote every morning for 90 minutes.

These 3 small mindset shifts made this easy (despite wanting to give up hundreds of times):

99% of people know exactly what they *should* be doing. 

And it's following a simple, boring process. 

But no one wants to hear that. 

So they spend time (and waste money) on complex "solutions." 

Luckily, this 2-minute read will help you avoid that trap:

Everything good in life comes from compounding.

And compounding comes from doing:

• The basics
• Every single day
• For a long period of time

Said differently:

• Simplicity
• Consistency
• Patience

But this is hard to stick to.

Compounding takes time.

So most people give up before seeing results.

(Maybe you've felt this – starting something with a ton of motivation but quitting when you didn't see immediate progress.)

Luckily, there's a solution:

People quit things for 1 reason:

Dopamine.

Well, it's a *lack* of dopamine.

You see, most people set a goal and think:

"I'll be happy once I achieve this goal."

And this leaves our simple human brains *starving* for dopamine along the way.

Here's what to do instead:

We stick to things we enjoy.

And we enjoy things that give us dopamine.

So we need to *somehow* get dopamine from the monotonous consistency that leads to compounding.

I call this Process Dopamine.

And here's a 3-step system for unlocking it (so you can achieve any goal):

First, stop setting goals – at least the way you're currently setting them.

Instead, choose an outcome.

Then, switch the goal to:

"I want to enjoy every second on the journey to achieving X."

Here's why this works:

It puts the focus on the *process* instead of the *outcome*.

With this as your focus, you can spend time figuring out how to make the *process* enjoyable, which makes achieving the *outcome* inevitable.

And here are 2 ways to love the process:

First, optimize for progress.

Find a way to make:

• Tiny, constant gains
• Every day or every week
• For long periods of time

Trust me – you will get addicted to tiny bits of progress (and stick with it forever).

This is the key to unlocking Process Dopamine.

By optimizing for progress, it's easy to stay motivated.

But, motivation won't last forever.

Along the way, there will be plenty of times where you don't feel like doing the things you know you need to do.

Which is where the last mindset shift comes in – the Daily Leapfrog.

Every day you take another step on the path of monotonous consistency, picture this:

There are thousands of people who pursued a similar goal, but quit on *this exact day* on their journey.

So picture yourself *leapfrogging* all of those people.

(Silly, but it works!)

To recap the Process Dopamine system:

1. Stop setting outcome goals. Instead, set goals to *enjoy every second* on the path to achieving the outcome

2. Optimize your path for consistent progress

3. Use the Daily Leapfrog to stay motivated on the most boring days

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: The bottleneck to your personal growth isn't "working harder."
entries.postBody:
The bottleneck to your personal growth isn't "working harder."

It's having the courage to:

• Work on something different
• Outside your comfort zone
• To acquire new skills

Which means transforming yourself into a different person.

People only change when the discomfort of not-changing exceeds their current comfort levels.

This is why so many people, once they get settled in a career, struggle so much to change directions.

And why people who have "nothing to lose" tend to be the ones who succeed.

So, how can you make being "uncomfortable," and moving forward, your new comfort zone?

That's something to journal on this morning.

entries.creator: Chris Donnelly
entries.hook: What skills are essential to master in 2025?
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
What skills are essential to master in 2025?

(Steal this to become future-proof)

The World Economic Forum published the Future of Jobs Report 2025, 
And the list of core skills for 2030 is absolutely fascinating.  

Key takeaways:  

📍 The top skills for 2030 are strategic, not technical.
📍 Analytical thinking tops the list.
📍 Success in 2030 will come down to how well you:

– Think through problems
– Build connections
– Invest in personal development 

If you’re a side hustler in 2025, aiming to be a founder by 2030... 
Study this list. 

These skills will directly shape your trajectory. 

Here's how to get ahead by 2030:   

1. AI and Big Data 🤖
↳ Subscribe to one AI or data-focused newsletter.
↳ Automate one aspect of your workflow (ChatGPT, RightMessage, ReadAI). 

2. Systems Thinking 🔧
↳ Map out a process you use weekly and one point of friction.
↳ Identify a system that can work even when you're at your 9-5. 

3. Creative Thinking 💭
↳ Commit to a 30-minute daily idea generation session.
↳ Try creating a cheat sheet on a topic in your niche. 

4. Talent Management 👥
↳ Document the roles you’d delegate first as your side hustle grows.
↳ Test outsourcing one small task now - even just a £25 UpWork brief.

5. Analytical Thinking 🤔
↳ Review data from your socials and think about how to action it.
↳ Choose one business metric and write down three inputs driving it.

6. Technological Literacy 🖥️
↳ Set a weekly learning goal in a particular IT skill.
↳ Dedicate 20 minutes every single day to work on this skill.

7. Empathy and Active Listening 👂
↳ Use your day job to sharpen communication with your colleagues.
↳ On your next customer call, take notes and ask specific follow-ups.  
	
8. Motivation and Self-Awareness 👤
↳ Track your energy levels throughout the day from 8 am to 8 pm.
↳ Schedule tasks according to energy, not time (I do deep work in the AM).

9. Leadership and Social Influence 🗣️
↳ Highlight a customer win or insight, not just the product.
↳ Post once a week on LinkedIn, sharing a principle behind your business.

10.	Curiosity and Lifelong Learning 🧠
↳ Listen to a podcast on a topic you're not familiar with. 
↳ Ask one person outside your field how they solve problems.

11.	Resilience, Flexibility, and Agility 🐆
↳ Write down one setback and what it taught you.
↳ Try one new approach this week to a repeating challenge. 

If you’re serious about building in the next 5 years,
Save this list.  

And if you want support while you scale...

I offer weekly business advice from 20+ years of entrepreneurship.
With the core strategies for scaling up without burning out.  
 
Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/eXSNaDiu

I have important lessons and 30+ free learning resources for you.

Which skill will you be focusing on first? 
Share it with me in the comments. 👇

♻️ Repost to help your network reach their goals.  
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

entries.creator: George Mack
entries.hook: The most creative ad copy of all time
entries.postBody:
The most creative ad copy of all time

21 examples: 

1. Depression awareness

Principle: The Small Text Reveal

Step 1 - Reader sees the big text

Step 2 - Reader thinks it's the sad looking person nearby

Step 3 - Reader sees the small text

Step 4 - Reader realizes Steffen is the person smiling in the distance


2. Land Rover

Principle: The U Turn 

Step 1 - Take them down a predictable path (Returns policy) 

Step 2 - U-turn to something they didn't predict


3. Nike

Principle: 4 Words

"If I had more time, I would have written a shorter letter" - Cicero


4. Daihatsu

Principle: The Reframe

Step 1 - Find a sexy product in the industry

Step 2 - Reframe the benefits so you're sexier than them


5. Wall Street Journal

Principle: Trim The Fat

4 words. Pure gold.

"If I had more time, I would have written a shorter letter"


6. Hyposwiss

Principle: Punchline First

Write the punchline: "They like us because of our honesty"

Then write the set up: "We like our clients because of their money"


7. New Balance

Principle: The Unexpected Connection


8. Fiverr

Principle: The Handbrake Turn

Use the headline to capture their attention...

And the small print to take a handbrake turn.


9. Transport For London

Principle: The Contrast Effect

Use the 1st sentence to create a small win.

Use the 2nd sentence to create a massive loss.

The 2nd sentence feels more extreme due to the contrast of the 1st sentence.


10. The Economist

Principle: Create an Anti-Customer


11. BMW

Principle: The Rule of 3

Use the 3rd line to take an unexpected left turn


12. Hinge

Principle: Call out the elephant in the industry


 13. AHM - Health Insurance

Principle: Lyric Hacking

Step 1: Find a song lyric everyone in the world knows

Step 2: Slot your product into it

I've got 99 problems but X ain't one.


14. Porsche 944S2

Principle: Hypnotic Identity Bias

If you don't think this car is exciting -- you must be dead. Therefore if you're not dead, you must think it's exciting.

The most hypnotic ad copy ever.


15. Reed Jobs

Principle: The Double Meaning

Looks like an ad about sex... Actually an ad for a job board.


16. Rolex

Principle: Sell The Dream... Then The Product

This is hypnosis:

"If you were achieving your dreams, you'd be wearing our product"


17. Lewis Capaldi

Principle: The Humble Brag

Balance out your bragging with self-deprecation.

People prefer 9/10 over 10/10.


18. Toronto City - Anti-littering ad

Principle: Fork The Common Phrase


19. Plum Holidays

Principle: Sell the YOLO

Everyone is procrastinating on their next holiday.

If you remind them how scarce life is, you create activation energy.


20. Toyota

Principle: The Original

If you can't advertise the latest sexy technology -- call yourself the original version.


21. BMW

Principle: Reverse Hypnosis

If you know about cars, you want a BMW. 

Therefore, if you don't want a BMW, you don't know about cars.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: I overthought my first 100 posts.
entries.postBody:
I overthought my first 100 posts.

Edited them to death.
Spent hours on each one.
Waited for the "perfect" moment to publish.

Most of them flopped anyway.

Then I started posting faster.

Rougher drafts. 
Less editing. 
Just hit publish and see what happens.

Those posts did better.

Turns out nobody cared about the stuff I was obsessing over.

They cared whether the idea landed.

Overthinking feels like effort.
But it's usually just fear.

You can't think your way to good content.
You have to publish your way there.

The feedback is in the arena, not in your drafts folder.

entries.hook: Losers play zero-sum games.
entries.postBody:
Losers play zero-sum games.

Winners root for everyone around them.

If you don't believe me, look at the vast majority of successful people on this platform.

They aren't jerks.
They don't play games.
They don't hate on each other.

They simply try their best to lift up everyone around them.

Is it a perfect data point? 
Do haters sometimes win?

Of course.

But I'm not interested in the outliers.
I'm interested in what happens 90% of the time.

Lead from the front, be optimistic and supportive and lift someone up when you see them accomplish something worth celebrating.

Be a karma entrepreneur.

Because even if you're successful as a hater, it's far more lonely when you get there, and nobody's there to congratulate you.

If this resonated with you, consider joining 180,000+ entrepreneurs who read my newsletter each week.

It's all about rethinking money, time, and what it means to live well.

And they never know how close they were.

Not overnight. Not all at once.

But it happened.

And it only happened because I didn't quit.

The people who succeed aren't the most talented.

They're the ones who stayed in the game long enough to win.

entries.hook: In 4 years, I went from being a broke freelance writer to generating over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter.
entries.postBody:
In 4 years, I went from being a broke freelance writer to generating over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter.

And I did it without these tools:

• AI
• A fancy website
• Or a complex funnel

But if I had to start from zero today, these are the 7 no-code tools I'd use as a ghostwriter:

1. Hemingway Editor

Gives your writing a grade and shows you where your content is:

• Hard to read
• Could be simplified
• Written in a passive voice

Now includes AI to rewrite content with your chosen tone.

2. Mem AI

AI-enhanced notes app that organizes client interviews:

• Summarizes notes automatically
• Writes content based on transcripts
• Recalls specific details across all notes

3. Wordable

Publishes from Google Docs to client websites with one click.

Works with WordPress, HubSpot, and Medium.

No more manual formatting or CMS headaches.

4. Notion

Your all-in-one workspace for:

• Client CRM tracking
• Content storage and proposals
• Project plans (with client access)

The Free plan works perfectly.

5. Loom

Record yourself and screen for:

• Client pitches
• Project explanations
• Content edit walkthroughs

The best way to delight your clients and reduce meetings.

6. Fathom

AI notetaker for Zoom, Google Meet, and Teams.

Auto-transcribes, summarizes, and creates action points.

Ask the AI chatbot questions about any meeting.

7. Kit (ConvertKit)

This is the perfect place to:

• Hold your own email list
• Build sample automations
• Send one-off emails and announcements to your list

As you grow as a ghostwriter, building your own email list is how to land more clients.

Then, later, use it to diversify your income with digital products.

These tools will completely change how you run your ghostwriting business.

—
📌 You can use these tools to become a top 1% ghostwriter.

But what service should you start with?

I recommend LinkedIn Ghostwriting.

So, I put together a 30-day roadmap to help you land your first (or next) LinkedIn ghostwriting client—with 7 AI prompts to make it 10x easier.

Want access?

Comment "roadmap" and I'll DM it to you.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: I have been in weekly therapy for over 10 years.
entries.postBody:
I have been in weekly therapy for over 10 years.

$100 per session
1x per week
4x per month
10 years

= $50,000

But here are 7 ways therapy has changed my life, and why I'd gladly pay 2x, 3x, 5x, 10x more all over again: 

-

Therapy gave me permission to explore how I felt.

The first 3+ years, all I did was rant and ramble about how I saw the world. 

And as a kid who didn't get this "space," being able to just talk and talk was all I needed.

And slowly, I started to be able to hear myself.

-

Therapy created distance between me and my worst feelings.

By being able to talk with someone else, consistently, every week, I started to see how I felt "objectively."

This gave me the opportunity to see them as separate from "myself," question them, & ultimately change them.

-

Therapy was a forcing function for accountability.

The moment I started, I knew I needed to go weekly.

Otherwise, I'd fall off.

And sure enough, when therapy rolled around every week, I was forced to rise to the occasion.

Slow steps, compounded over a long period of time.

-

Therapy allowed me to move from Reactive to Proactive.

When I first started therapy, it was to "work through" past pain.

But as I learned about myself and grew, therapy became a proactive exercise in anticipating future struggles.

And getting ahead of them.

-

Therapy taught me compassion.

"You can only meet others as far as you've met yourself."

The more I did this inner work, the more I understood other people and why they were the way they were.

I could see they were afraid to change.

B/c I had been afraid too.

-

Therapy gave me a foundation of first principles emotional skills.

Despite years of therapy, life still challenges me (and all of us) often.

But when things happen, at least now I have tools I can use to reconnect within myself again.

And operate from a place of clarity.

-

Therapy has made me a more effective, driven individual.

I didn't know this would be an outcome—and most people I talk to don't either.

But I attribute the MAJORITY of my success in life to my years in therapy.

B/c now I understand where the choices I make come from in myself.

I now pay $500/hr for weekly therapy, and have continued seeking out more and more knowledgeable people to work with and learn from.

And as I make more and achieve more in life, I will likely pay even more.

It's a non-negotiable cost.

Even when I was poor, I still paid for it.

One of my goals in life is to help de-stigmatize therapy and inner work, especially for men.

Because especially in business, most men I know ignore this work completely.

They achieve external success, but remain emotionally stunted.

Which limits what they do with their $$$.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: In the last 5 years, I've lost 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and built a writing business I love.
entries.postBody:
In the last 5 years, I've lost 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and built a writing business I love.

But it's hard to see how far you've come when you're reaching for the next goal.

So I use these 11 "Indicators Of Growth" to remind myself of the progress I've made:

1. Having problems my former self would beg to have

2. Talking to people I once greatly looked up to

3. Doing output I once found extremely difficult

4. Saying no to opportunities I would have once immediately taken on

5. Having daily habits I used to struggle to stick with

6. No longer speaking with people who I once talked to daily

7. Ignoring the opinions of today I used to get caught up thinking about

8. Reacting rationally in situations I once found overwhelming

9. Having knowledge in a domain in which I used to be clueless

10. Being okay not having something I once put on a pedestal

11. Having something today former me once yearned to have

That's it!

If you liked this, repost it so we can share these indicators with more people. 

And if you want more no-fluff frameworks on personal progress, then you'll love my newsletter.

Sign up for free here: https://dickiesdigest.com/

entries.hook: At Steve Jobs's memorial service, all 500 guests were given one book:
entries.postBody:
At Steve Jobs's memorial service, all 500 guests were given one book:

Jobs had reread it every year for 38 years.

In 1972, while in high school, Steve Jobs discovered the "Autobiography of a Yogi" by Paramahansa Yogananda.

In Yogananda's teachings, Jobs found the secret to channeling consciousness into creativity:

• He meditated daily
• Became a vegetarian
• Studied Zen Buddhism

And these 5 ideas from "Autobiography of a Yogi" helped him build the $1 trillion Apple empire:

1. Self-realization.

Steve Jobs was drawn to the spiritual disciplines of Kriya Yoga:

• Transcend limitations
• Access intuitive wisdom
• Manifest your fullest potential

These routines unlocked Jobs's inner genius.

2. Yogananda's "no limitations" message became Jobs's leadership mantra.

For Jobs, Apple products must:

• Be different 
• Push boundaries
• Disrupt industries

Macs, iPods, and iPhones all came from a mind expanded by meditation.

3. Yogananda helped Jobs tap into his intuition.

He described it as "more powerful than intellect" and credited it for his biggest breakthroughs.

Through daily meditation, Jobs strengthened his intuitive muscles.

4. Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication.

Yogananda once said: 

"Be as simple as you can be; you will be astonished to see how uncomplicated and happy your life can become."

Jobs's obsession with simplicity came from his pursuit of inner clarity.

5. Passion is born of a deep sense of purpose.

Jobs had a relentless drive to put a "dent in the universe."

And to do that, he needed to be passionate. Because if he didn't have passion for what he was doing, he wouldn't have been able to continue building Apple, even when times got tough.

Jobs always saw himself as part of the spiritual lineage of innovators like Yogananda.

And his dying wish for the world echoes the core message of the yogi whose words shaped his life.

"You can't connect the dots looking forward," he famously said. "Only backwards."

Yogananda's memoir was his map.

-
📌 If you want to write LinkedIn posts and stories like this for industry leaders, then become a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I made my first $10,000 on the internet.

Here's a free, 5-day crash course to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ep3kMFAA

entries.hook: A stupid simple way to stand out in business (and life):
entries.postBody:
A stupid simple way to stand out in business (and life):

Be extremely easy to work with.

Be an optimist.
Be a pleasant person.
Respond quickly to requests.
Always do what you said you'd do.

That's the basics.
That's really the whole strategy.

An alarming number of people are bad at all of these things.

They're pessimistic.
They're miserable to be around.
They take weeks or months to respond.
They drop the ball on every major project.

Then, when that eventually catches up to them (it always does), they tell the whole world that everyone else is the problem.

I've worked with a lot of incredible people over 23 years.

The ones I stay in touch with had a few things in common:

They were excellent at their job.
They made my life (and MY job) easier.

Want to build a better life and business?

Clear that bar.

It's a competitive advantage that costs you nothing but a little time and extra energy.

If this kind of thinking resonates, I write a weekly newsletter you might enjoy.

Counterintuitive ideas on earning money, reclaiming your time, and building a life you choose.

Join 200,000+ ambitious readers here: https://lnkd.in/gkf8cwy8

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: 10 ways to stand out in a hiring process (that don’t involve your resume):
entries.postBody:
10 ways to stand out in a hiring process (that don’t involve your resume):

1 - Do Your Research

Before an interview, spend a few hours researching the company and role. At a minimum, you should cover: Company mission, recent company news, recent market news, backgrounds of leaders, and backgrounds of interviewers. Google is a powerful asset—use it.

2 - Embrace “I Don’t Know”

You can’t know the answer to every question. And you know what? That’s ok. Don’t be afraid to say “I don’t know”—but follow it with a plan to acquire that information. Ex: “I don’t know, but I’ll dig in and follow up via email.” Then actually follow up!

3 - Leverage Warm Intros

Warm intros are the holy grail of a competitive hiring process. Scan your networks for any connections to a company—use LinkedIn for this!

4 - Prepare for “Why Us?”

Interviewers inevitably ask, “Why us?”—make sure you're prepared for it. Leverage your research. Write down 2-3 unique points about the company that appeal to you. The more specific, the better.

5 - Pass the Plane Test

There’s a common—and dated!—test in the hiring process: “Would I want to sit next to this person on a plane for 6 hours?” This was about being “normal”—but normalcy is overrated. Be yourself, but be sure to get across that you're kind and genuine.

 6 - Cite Real Weaknesses

Getting asked about your weaknesses feels like a trap, so we tend to cite weaknesses that could be viewed as strengths. “I’m TOO detail oriented." I once told a finance interviewer I didn’t know accounting—but that I would learn it. That’s a real weakness!

7 - Highlight Learning as a Goal

When asked about your goals, always highlight learning. Lifelong learners tend to be great employees—they're interested, intellectually curious, and driven to do more than what is asked of them. Emphasize your focus on lifelong learning.

8 - Ask Unique Questions

Most interviews end with a classic: “Do you have any questions for me?” This isn’t a throwaway. It's an opportunity to show off your differentiated initiative. Ask a unique question grounded in your research on the company.

9 - Personalized Thank Yous

After an interview, always make it a point to send a thank you note to the interviewer. It should be concise and direct. Include a specific, personalized detail from the interview—something you found interesting or a key follow up.

10 - Stop Fearing Rejection

I’m not ashamed to admit that I’ve been rejected for more jobs than I can count. It happens—you can’t be a fit for everything and everyone. Stop fearing rejection and put yourself out there.

**

Those are 10 ways to stand out in a hiring process that don't involve your resume. What others would you add to the list?

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: 1,012 tech companies have laid off workers in 2023.
entries.postBody:
1,012 tech companies have laid off workers in 2023. 
 
21,000 Meta employees - cut. 
 
12,000 Google employees - gone. 
 
The "safe" job might not be as safe as you think it is. 
 
And some people are pissed off. 
 
But behind the anger, there's a bigger opportunity. 
 
An opportunity to prepare. 
 
Because when profit is down, shareholders are panicked, and board members are upset, layoffs are the next thing coming. 
 
One moment you're a valuable piece of the "family", and then suddenly you aren't. 
 
That sucks. 
 
No matter how loved you feel, no matter how close you are to your boss, no matter how much they rolled out the red carpet...in the end? 
 
It's a math decision. 
 
You're a number on a spreadsheet. 
 
I've been there. 
 
Is it fair? Maybe not. 
Is it true? 100% 
 
The earlier you realize this in your career, the better off you are. 
 
So start future-proofing yourself. 
 
If you're fortunate enough to be employed, start building out your online brand, share your thoughts & learnings, and engage with other talented people. 
 
Create small side hustles and projects. 
 
Network like crazy. Make yourself impossible to forget. 
 
Become synonymous with something that can generate a little income should you need it. Anything. 
 
If you've been let go from your job, don't put the "Open for Work" badge on your profile. 
 
Instead, go create something: 
 
- Attention 
- Content 
- Software 
- A newsletter 
- A blog 
- Audio 
- Video 
 
Whatever. Just create! 
 
Don't tell people you're open for work. SHOW them you're worth working with. 
 
Stand out. 
Make noise. 
Be different. 
Be in as many places as you can be. 
 
And when you get hired again, please don't stop. 
 
Always have a plan B. 
 
Always be prepared.

Here’s 2 ways to start: https://lnkd.in/eGzFY6Tg

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: True wealth isn't in your wallet.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
True wealth isn't in your wallet.

It's the time you spend living life.

I've downgraded my life several times:

Fewer possessions.
Simpler business.
Easier routine.

Each subtraction added more luxury.

What I gained:

- Days with room to think.
- Time to think without interruption.
- Sleep that actually feels like sleep.
- The ability to be present with my wife.

But, we're sold the opposite: 

More meetings, faster responses, constant updates, bigger everything.

But here's the thing:

I might make less money when I create less content.

I might miss "opportunities" when I don't respond to DMs.

I might fall behind the new trends when I read books, not feeds.

But I don't care.

I'm not interested in being busy and important. 

I want the freedom *not* to be.

What small thing could you subtract today to add a little luxury to your life?

Delete it.

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: My grandfather told me the story of the Blind Men and the Elephant:
entries.postBody:
My grandfather told me the story of the Blind Men and the Elephant:

(everyone needs to hear this)

Six blind men are brought to examine an elephant that has come to their village.

The first man touches the trunk and says that the elephant is like a thick snake.

The second man touches the tusk and says that the elephant is like a spear.

The third man touches the ear and says that the elephant is like a fan.

The fourth man touches the leg and says that the elephant is like a tree.

The fifth man touches the side and says the elephant is like a wall.

The sixth man touches the tail and says the elephant is like a rope.

Each of the blind men is convinced that he is right and that everyone else is wrong.

American poet John Godfrey Saxe’s version of the story concludes with a simple, beautiful line:

Each was partly in the right, And all were in the wrong.​

So, oft in theologic wars, The disputants, I ween, Rail on in utter ignorance, Of what each other mean, And prate about an Elephant, Not one of them has seen!

The moral of this story is that the information we have about the world represents a tiny fraction of the information available, yet we use it to form a view of how the world works.

The world is always infinitely more complex than our narrow observation windows would allow us to understand.

I'm reminded of David Foster Wallace's famous This Is Water speech in 2005:

"The point here is that I think this is one part of what teaching me how to think is really supposed to mean. To be just a little less arrogant. To have just a little critical awareness about myself and my certainties. Because a huge percentage of the stuff that I tend to be automatically certain of is, it turns out, totally wrong and deluded."

You can either choose to (A) cling to the notion that your view is the absolute truth or (B) embrace a degree of humility about your views and open your mind to change.

The goal of Path A is to preserve the notion of our own intelligence. The goal of Path B is to broaden and improve our view of reality by acquiring new and better information.

We should all choose Path B...

"It ain't what you don't know that gets you into trouble. It's what you know for sure that just ain't so." ― Mark Twain

At the end of every quarter, ask yourself what you've changed your mind on recently.

If you can't think of anything, that's a bad thing.

Fighting back against perspective blindness and opening your mind to new ideas is a tried and true path to consistent, compounding growth.

The next time you want to reject an alternative viewpoint without thinking, remember the story of the Blind Men and the Elephant:

The world is much more complex than any of us can possibly understand.

***

If you enjoyed this or learned something, follow me Sahil Bloom for more in future.

entries.hook: Energy doesn't lie.
entries.postBody:
Energy doesn't lie.

Listen to it.

When something is right for you, it will give you energy.

That's how you know.

Not because it's easy.
Not because it's comfortable.

But because it lights you up!

Most people ignore this.

They chase things that drain them.

The job that pays well but kills their soul...
The project that looks good but feels heavy...

And they wonder why they're exhausted.

Energy is the signal.

When I started writing online, it gave me energy.

Not because it was easy. It wasn't.
Not because I was good at it. I wasn't.

But it felt right.

Every piece of content, even the bad ones, made me want to write the next one.

That's how I knew.

Compare that to corporate.

Every email felt heavy.
Every meeting drained me.
Every project took more than it gave.

Same effort. 
Different energy.

One was right for me. One wasn't.

If you're doing work that drains you, maybe it's not your work.

If you're ready to build something that gives you energy instead of taking it, 45,000+ students (including 200+ LinkedIn Top Voices) used the LinkedIn Operating System to find work that feels right, talk about it, and grow an audience that cares about it.

You can watch it here: https://buff.ly/soidgrf

Energy doesn't lie.

Follow it.

entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: In 1991, Warren Buffett gifted Bill Gates his favorite book.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
In 1991, Warren Buffett gifted Bill Gates his favorite book.

Gates read it and said:

"This is the best business book I've ever read."

Business Adventures was written in 1969 by John Brooks.

It’s a collection of 12 real-life stories about ambition, failure, greed, and resilience.

Warren Buffett read Business Adventures in the 1960s, and it stuck with him for decades.

Each story in the book is a masterclass in:

• Psychology
• Leadership
• And decision-making

These are my 3 favorite stories & lessons:

1. Markets Are Psychological, Not Logical

On May 28, 1962, a stock market crash sent Wall Street into chaos.

The NYSE ticker ran 82 minutes behind. Trading volume hit 9.35M shares.

Men like Keith Funston, NYSE president, had no answers when reporters asked what was happening:

• No war had started
• No banks had failed
• No pandemic had broken out

Just pure fear spreading faster than the facts.

The S&P 500 dropped 22.5% over six months, and the Dow fell 5.7% on May 28 alone.

The market only recovered after the end of the Cuban Missile Crisis eased investor fears.

2. Vision Beats Validation

In 1957, Ford launched the Edsel after spending $250 million on development.

• 18 consumer research studies
• 2 years of focus groups
• Custom styling

Ford was sure they had created the perfect car.

But by 1960, Ford had discontinued the Edsel after losing $350 million.

Their market research missed something obvious:

Americans' tastes had shifted to smaller cars.

The data told them what customers *said*, not what they'd actually *buy*.

The lesson? 

Bold vision beats excessive validation when markets are changing.

3. Experience Transforms Skeptics Into Believers

In 1959, Xerox launched the 914 copier:

• IBM had rejected the technology as unmarketable in 1955
• Banks refused loans, calling it "too complicated" for offices
• It cost $27,500—the same as 5 cars

So Xerox got creative: 

Leasing cost of $95/month for 2,000 copies + 5-cent per copy.

Office managers quickly found countless uses for the machine. By 1965, Xerox copiers were producing millions of copies daily nationwide.

Now you know why "try before you buy" works.

In Business Adventures, Brooks' storytelling made complex business ideas unforgettable.

And this is why leaders need writers to turn their insights into relatable stories.

I experienced this firsthand when I made my first $10,000 online as a ghostwriter.

My clients needed someone to tell their stories the way Brooks explained these lessons.

Why?

Because industry leaders want to scale online but don't have the time to write.

This is why ghostwriting is such a lucrative writing opportunity.

Want to land high-paying ghostwriting clients?

This free, 5-day email course will show you how (so you can get paid to write & work with leaders in your niche).

Get instant access here: https://lnkd.in/eFEwtp_C

entries.hook: Something I wish I knew about much sooner:
entries.postBody:
Something I wish I knew about much sooner:

The 8+8+8 Rule.

Without realising it, most people's time management is misaligned.

Some focus far too much on their job or business.
Others put too much energy into anything but.

It hurts their growth and limits their potential.

The truth is, you need a good balance of everything.
Which is where The 8+8+8 Rule comes in:

🧑🏻‍💻 8 Hours Of Hard Work:
↳ Dedicate 8 hours to your professional duties and enter truly deep focus.
↳ If you are highly efficient, this is more than enough time.

🛌 8 Hours Of Good Sleep:
↳ Sleep is the foundation that absolutely everything else is built on.
↳ As Bryan Johnson says: "Build your life around sleep. No greater ROI anywhere.” 

🧑‍🧑‍🧒 8 Hours On The 3Fs, 3Hs, & 3Ss:
↳ Family, friends, and faith - whatever that looks like for you.
↳ Health, hygiene, and hobby - try to mix these 3 into 1.
↳ Soul, service, and smile - because helping others helps you.

Once you adopt this approach, you:

📈 Increase your productivity.
🧠 Improve your mental health.
🌍 Live a more purposeful life.

This isn't a fad.

I started using this rule years ago, and still use it today.
It might seem strange to implement at first, but it's worth the awkwardness.

So, if you find yourself lacking the balance you want in life...
Give The 8+8+8 Rule a try.

What do you think of The 8+8+8 Rule?
Would love to have a discussion in the comments ⬇️

♻️ Repost to help your network find balance.
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

If you want to learn more about actionable frameworks like this one...
It's something I discuss frequently in my newsletter, Step by Step:

https://lnkd.in/eXSNaDiu

There are 30+ free learning resources waiting for you as well.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Every big change in my life started the same way —
entries.postBody:
Every big change in my life started the same way —

I was afraid.

Quitting my job? Terrifying.
Moving to the mountains? Uncomfortable.
Starting my business? Felt impossible.

But fear shouldn't be a stop sign.

Think of it as an arrow.

The phone call you don’t want to make.
The idea that won’t leave you alone.
The move that feels risky.

They’re not obstacles.
They’re invitations.

So, I try and follow a simple rule:

If something makes me nervous, I try my best to lean in.

Not because I like fear. I don't.

But because I know what’s on the other side — something great.

What you resist gets bigger.
What you confront gets smaller.

What's scaring you right now?

Maybe that's exactly where you need to go next.

---

Enjoy this?

If so, learn how, through writing, I've built an audience of 700K+ followers and 375.6M+ impressions on LinkedIn: https://lnkd.in/eh9pVVuf

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: My honest advice to someone who wants to make a lot of money. 3 things I wish I knew at 22:
entries.postBody:
My honest advice to someone who wants to make a lot of money. 3 things I wish I knew at 22:

1. The only way to make a lot of money is to create a lot of value.

Here's a harsh truth: No one hands out money. No one is going to pay you just because they like you or think you're cool. That's not the way the world works.
Money earned is a direct byproduct of value created.

Create value, receive value. If money is the goal, value has to be the focus.

This isn't just some vague idea: The only way to get rich is to create an enormous amount of value for others, and capture a small portion of that along the way.

It's not talking about the thing, it's not brainstorming about the thing, it's not asking about the thing, it's not thinking about the thing.

The only way to create value is by doing the thing.

And if you don't know where to start, look around you. Customers, colleagues, bosses, shareholders, employees. Every single one of them has a problem. What problems can you solve for the people around you?

Figure them out, solve them, scale that solution.

That's how you make money.

2. You have to demonstrate excellence in everything you do.

Your income scales proportional to the amount of excellence that you are able to demonstrate. This is particularly true if you're going to work your way up at someone else's company.

It's simple: If you want to get promoted, earn the big raise, get that fancy title, and more, then demonstrate excellence in everything you do.

Everything matters. Every single thing. You don't get to pick and choose when to show up, because the world will ignore your best and judge you for your worst. Top performers show up with energy and enthusiasm for the little things just as much as they do for the big things.

If you're in the top-10% of performers, there's no ceiling for what you can do. But the self-awareness to identify where you currently stack up, and to hear and adapt to the honest feedback on it, is very rare.

If you're in the top-10%, you know it. If you're not, figure out why and fix it.

3. You don't need passion, you need energy.

I've always taken issue with the advice to follow your passion.

You don't have to be passionate about your professional pursuits, you just need to find energy in them. You just need to feel a pull towards them. You just need to feel that spark of curiosity in them.

Passion can lie—but energy never does.

When you have energy for something, you're prone to giving your deep attention to learn more about it, to ask the right questions, to figure it out, to win.

Go deeper on this advice and more in my NYT bestselling book, The 5 Types of Wealth. It's 30% off on Amazon, so if you're interested in grabbing a copy (or gifts for people in your life), now's a great time to take advantage.

Order here: https://lnkd.in/eUzjZUxj

P.S. Email the receipt to sahil@sahilbloom.com and I'll send you a 50+ page companion workbook to guide your reading.

entries.hook: I studied fiction writing in college.
entries.structural: Question post, Story post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
I studied fiction writing in college.

But I always tell people I didn't learn how to write in college.

I learned how to read, and I learned the importance of reading my work out loud.

Here's a mini-masterclass in what to HEAR in your own writing:

-

1/ Listen for tone. 

As soon as the words start to come out of your mouth, you will feel what tone you were writing in.

The big question is: was this tone your intention?

If yes, you'll hear what gives this tone its quality. Do more of that.

If no, you'll hear why not.

-

2/ Listen for wordiness.

"And so it was then that..."

Anytime you hear lots of tiny words next to hear other, you'll feel your mouth fumble over them.

Don't ignore this.

Cut them. Compress them.

Your writing will be sharper.

-

3/ Listen for off-brand language.

"I looked at her with glimmering eyes..."

The moment an off-brand description, word, or phrase falls out of your mouth, you will cringe in pain.

It'll be so potently apparent that isn't you, or isn't the real voice of the character.

Cut it.

-

4/ Listen for pace.

Long descriptions seem great when they're in your head.

But read them out loud and you'll feel yourself getting bored of yourself.

Don't discount this feeling.

The moment you start zoning out, the description has died.

Compress it. Get to the point.

-

5/ Listen for your own emotions.

Reading your writing out loud makes it real.

In emotional moments, you'll feel your chest tighten.

In vulnerable moments, you'll feel your throat close.

In hilarious moments, you'll let out a laugh.

These = your BEST writing.

-

Reading your writing out loud is the most important part of becoming a great writer.

You will learn much more about yourself and how to improve just by speaking the words.

All inadequacies reveal themselves, in the best way.

Now you know what to fix.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: The most successful people I know are a bit nutty.
entries.postBody:
The most successful people I know are a bit nutty.

They believe they can build anything.
They know their weird idea will work.
They think they'll compete with $1B+ companies...

Everyone else calls them crazy.

"That market is already too crowded."
"You need experience in that industry first."
"You can't start a business without funding."

But here's the thing about "crazy" people:

They don't listen to the noise.

They start anyway.
They build anyway.
They work anyway.

While rational people are making spreadsheets and conducting market research, crazy people are already selling their first product.

The business world needs more delusional optimists.

People who know they can win.
Who believe their voice matters.
Who are willing to look silly while building.

Every major company started with someone who was "crazy enough" to think they could do it better.

It all started with people who ignored the experts telling them it wouldn't work.

Your "crazy" idea might be exactly what the world needs.

Stop listening to reasonable people giving you reasonable advice.

Start building something unreasonable.

Ready to turn your "crazy" idea into a profitable business?

I've helped 45,000+ people start talking about and marketing their ideas right here on LinkedIn.

(inlcuding 200+ LinkedIn Top Voices)

Here's the entire playbook: https://buff.ly/ALO2j0P

Once you watch it, go promote that new idea and see what happens.

entries.creator: Codie Sanchez
entries.hook: Look at this guy.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, How-to / Framework post
entries.postBody:
Look at this guy.

He started a garbage business in ~15 days.

His background? A software entrepreneur.

Insane all the way around.

Here's how he's doing it:

Spencer Scott saw an opportunity when his fancy neighborhood was pissed about their new trash service.

He threw up a post on Facebook as a test:
Were people serious enough about their complaints to switch providers?

His post got 100s of likes & comments. So he made a simple offer:

If 200 people signed up at $99 for 3 months, he'd start a new trash company.

150 people ended up signing up.

In 10 days, he had $15k in the bank. That was enough to get started...

[...continues with the operational details: $40k truck off Facebook Marketplace, 200 used bins for $35 each from Michigan, $2k monthly opex, $150K bad-year / $500K good-year revenue at 20% margins, one truck running one day a week...]

So what can we learn from Spencer's garbage success?

5 trashy yet classy lessons:

1. Find pain points in stagnant industries.

Trash, landscaping, cleaning - these industries are ripe for disruption. Look where customers are fed up with bad service & pounce on the opportunity.

2. Presell to validate your idea and fund your startup costs.

[...continues with: focus on recurring revenue, acquire undervalued assets, build with exit in mind...]

5. Build with an exit in mind.

From day one, Spencer knew trash companies were acquisition targets. So he built his business to be sold.

↓ ↓ ↓

All this just scratches the surface. Watch the video I did with Spencer here for the full story → https://lnkd.in/eM4kTs_7

entries.creator: Chris Donnelly
entries.hook: You're not born a natural problem-solver.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
You're not born a natural problem-solver.

It's a skill that needs developing with time:

Especially if you want to build a successful digital business.  

Most people don't realise it, 
But a founder's job is mostly just problem solving on repeat...
  
Day in and day out.  

Over the last few years, I’ve used different problem-solving models
Depending on what needed my attention:  

💸 Keeping revenue consistent and predictable.
🔧 Setting a strategy that’s clear and actionable.
⭐️ Building a culture people actually want to be part of. 
⚙️ Running smooth operations, even when I’m not in the room.

As you can imagine, each one requires a completely different approach. 

These are the four models that I return to most often 👇

🔍 First Principles Thinking
↳ Strip everything back and start from zero. 

1. What do I know for sure about this problem?
2. What’s just a habit or assumption — not a fact?
3. If I had to build a solution from zero, what would it look like?
4. What if I forgot how this is “usually done”?
5. What’s the simplest possible version of solving this?

🔄 Second-Order Thinking
↳ Zoom out and see the bigger picture. 

1. If this works... what else does it trigger?
2. What does this look like in 6 months? 2 years?
3. Am I solving a short-term pain or creating a long-term problem?
4. What unintended consequences could show up later?
5. What would someone smarter than me worry about here?

🧠 Root Cause Analysis
↳ Fix an entire system, not just a symptom. 

1. What exactly went wrong — and when?
2. What’s the first thing that caused this to break down?
3. If I asked “why?” five times… where would I end up?
4. Where have we solved this badly before?
5. What keeps making this problem reappear?

⚡️ The OODA Loop
↳ When you just need to take the leap. 

1. What’s actually happening right now — no bias, just facts?
2. What do I need to unlearn before I can move forward?
3. Based on what I know, what’s the smartest next decision?
4. What small test can I run immediately?
5. What would I change if I had to act in the next 10 minutes?

It's easy to panic when an issue arises, 
But it will do nothing to actually solve the problem. 

To problem solve like the top 1%, 
You need to stop reacting emotionally...

And start responding strategically. 

If you want to stay sharp under pressure, 
My weekly newsletter will help you solve real business problems. 

Join Step by Step and get actionable insights every Sunday.👇
https://lnkd.in/eXSNaDiu

I have other important lessons and 30+ free learning resources for you.

What major problem did you solve recently, and how?
Share your story in the comments. ⬇️

♻️ Repost to help your network become better problem-solvers. 
And follow Chris Donnelly for more.

entries.creator: Nicolas Cole
entries.hook: Drinking my coffee & reflecting this morning.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post, Personal essay / Reflection
entries.postBody:
Drinking my coffee & reflecting this morning.

A few thoughts on cultivating an abundance mindset:

-

I feel least creative when I am "competing" with others.

I do my worst work when I am trying to "beat" someone else.

-

A mentor of mine once asked me:

"Do you love to win? Or hate to lose?"

How you answer this question reveals how you see the world.

An abundance mindset = running your own race.

• If you "love to win," that means others can too.

• But if you "hate to lose," that means life is a zero-sum game.

There can only be 1 winner.

This is a scarcity mindset.

-

In a scarcity mindset, the world has a fixed number of:

• Opportunities
• Resources
• Friendships/relationships
• Money
• Fame/attention

Your job is to then "compete" over the above.

Which means your entire headset is rooted in being "better" than the next person.

But in an abundance mindset, there is no "lack."

• You can create net-new opportunities
• You can share friendships
• You can generate more money together
• You can compound fame/attention
• You can invent resources

Your "competition" becomes your collaborators.

-

When you live your life with an abundance mindset: 

1+1 = 3

It's not *just* that you can create/achieve more together.

It's that you can unlock opportunities you otherwise would never be able to alone.

Life is not a 1-player game.

Some rewards require multiple players.

-

How do you cultivate an abundance mindset?

When you compare, consider how you can collaborate.

When you are jealous, consider what you can learn.

When you want to fight, consider how you can be friends.

When you want respect, consider how you can give it first.

When you want to prove how smart you are, consider the benefits of being "the dumbest person in the room."

When you are let down or betrayed, consider the lesson you can share with others.

When you are succeeding, consider how you can throw down a rope to the next person.

-

Those who measure their success in "things," titles, trophies, or achievements, tend to live life through a scarcity mindset.

They love they won—but they love even more that someone else lost.

But those who measure their success in impact, and how many people they've helped achieve their own personal transformation, tend to live life through an abundance mindset.

They love they won.

But they love that others won even more.

entries.hook: I wrote a 70,000-word novel with ChatGPT.
entries.structural: Question post, List post, Story post
entries.postBody:
Nicolas Cole 🚢👻 reposted this

I wrote a 70,000-word novel with ChatGPT.

Parts were great, parts were horrible.

Here's what I learned:

***

I had two questions I wanted to answer?

1. Can AI help me write faster? 

2. Can AI write a quality book? 

The first got a Yes, the second a definitive No.

But my biggest takeaway after countless hours testing:

AI is an accelerant, not a replacement.

Am I terrified of AI? A little bit.

Am I also excited about it? Yeah.

Rather than avoid it, I want to get great with it.

Thoughts?

***

If you want to use AI to accelerate your writing, I've got something for you:

• Execute advanced World Building like a pro
• Use the 3-Act Structure to outline your story from start to finish
• Leverage Plot Archetypes to tell proven stories (guaranteed to hook readers)

Nicolas Cole 🚢🏴‍☠️ and I call it '90-Minute Novel.'

Check it out: https://lnkd.in/gd3sCfgG

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Don't network like an amateur. Instead, try this:
entries.postBody:
Don't network like an amateur. 
 
Instead, try this: 
 
1. Present yourself clearly: 
 
- Who are you? 
- What do you do? 
- Why pay attention to you? 
 
2. Use a value-driven approach: 
 
- Solve their problem. 
- Send them something useful. 
- Support their business/project. 
 
3. Become a connector: 
 
- Facilitate introductions 
- Help them level up their network 
- Become the go to 'connector' 
 
Most people mess these up. 
 
They make it all about 'Me! Me! Me!'  
 
Wrong. 
 
Building relationships is about THEM. 
 
If you're struggling to network, read my free guide: https://lnkd.in/e2Z859PG 
 
Then, build that networking muscle. 
 
~~~ 
 
✍️ What's your best tip? 
♻️ Reshare if these are helpful.

entries.creator: Sahil Bloom
entries.hook: This philosophy changed my life.
entries.structural: Question post, How-to / Framework post, Story post
entries.postBody:
This philosophy changed my life.

(it's only 3 words)

"Use the Difficulty"

Here's how Michael Caine discovered it:

As a young actor, he was rehearsing a play scene when a chair got stuck in the door and blocked his path.

He told the other actor he couldn't get by the chair to enter the room in the scene.

The actor's response:

"Use the difficulty...if it's a comedy, fall over it, if it's a drama, pick it up and smash it."

This idea—of using the difficulty—became a defining mantra for Michael Caine's life.

"There's never anything so bad that you cannot use that difficulty...if you can use it a quarter of one percent to your advantage, you're ahead, you didn't let it get you down."

This idea hit me hard...

How can you use the difficulty you're currently facing?

How can you embrace the struggle?

How can you find flow through the friction?

How can you sow the seeds of your light during the periods of darkness?

As with everything in life, control the controllable:

The difficulty is already there, you can't control it.

You can control how you react to it.

You can control your response to it.

You can control your attitude towards it.

Lesson: Difficulty is inevitable. Use it.

📌 Want the books that built my best systems and ideas?

Download my Favorite Books PDF (free) and join 800,000+ who get my newsletter: https://lnkd.in/eSrGb8Kr
