entries.creator: Dickie Bush
entries.hook: Over the last 5 years, I've read 100+ books on writing.
entries.postBody:
Over the last 5 years, I've read 100+ books on writing.

But 90% of them were a waste of time.

Read these and throw the rest away:

1. The Adweek Copywriting Handbook by Eugene Schwartz

The only copywriting book you'll ever need – full stop. 

Learn the art of capturing & keeping attention.

2. Writing That Works by Kenneth Roman and Joel Raphaelson

This is the only business writing book you need.

• Emails
• Resumes
• Letters
• Messages
• Speeches

This book will teach you how to craft anything business-related (in a way people will actually read).

3. The War of Art by Steven Pressfield

Writing is a daily battle with the Resistance.

But until you're aware of it, the Resistance will dominate you.

This book single-handled changed my life – once you read it, you'll never see creative work the same way.

4. On Writing Well by William Zinsser

The no-BS guide to writing effective non-fiction.

Filled with examples of classic mistakes that once you see, you'll never make again.

5. Atomic Habits by James Clear

This book has nothing to do with writing.

But you can use the four principles in this book to build your writing habit:

• Make it obvious
• Make it attractive
• Make it easy
• Make it satisfying

6. Show Your Work by Austin Kleon

The idea of the writer slaving away for months in solitude is no longer true.

The best writers are sharing all of their ideas in public, getting feedback, and iterating.

7. The Elements of Style by William Strunk

The most tactical book on this list. 

• Active vs. passive voice
• Positive vs. negative form
• Concise vs. verbose writing
• Lists of words to never use again

Keep this one on your desk.

8. The Boron Letters by Gary Halbert

Read this book for 1 reason:

To study Gary Halbert's incredible writing style.

This guy has an incredible way with words – a voice, rhythm, and tone that keeps your eyes glued to the page.

9. THE book that completely broke my brain about what it meant to write online:

The Art and Business of Online Writing by Nicolas Cole

• Why you shouldn't start a blog
• Generating an endless amount of ideas
• The "Golden Intersection" of telling personal stories

Truly epic.

That's it!

What would you add to this list? Let me know in the replies.

entries.hook: The quickest way to make $10,000 per month as a writer:
entries.postBody:
The quickest way to make $10,000 per month as a writer:

(Even if you're a complete beginner)

Ghostwriting.

I've generated over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter.

But if I had to start from zero today, I'd pick one of these 5 ghostwriting services:

1. Social Ghostwriting

This is about scaling someone's insights & knowledge to:

• Boost their personal brand
• Attract customers to their business

Usually, you write for a founder, investor, or business owner.

And each client pays you between $2,000 to $4,000+ per month.

The best place to start?

Ghostwriting on LinkedIn.

The demand for LinkedIn ghostwriters has never been higher (and it keeps growing!).

So, I put together a free, 30-Day roadmap (with 7 AI prompts) to help you:

• Niche down
• Write high-quality LinkedIn content
• And pitch & land clients (without being salesy)

Comment "roadmap" and I will DM it to you.

2. Educational Email Course Ghostwriting

Most websites & social content are not converting the attention they generate.

Or they're doing it poorly.

The solution?

An Educational Email Course (EEC).

This is a free opt-in made up of a series of emails delivering valuable content over time.

And clients will pay you $5,000+ per EEC.

3. Weekly Newsletter Ghostwriting

Newsletters aren't just about "staying top of mind."

They are a compounding asset for clients:

• Creates a library of content
• Increases conversion likelihood
• Builds trust through consistent value delivery

Clients will pay you $3,000 to $10,000 per month for this service.

4. Ghostwriting Thought Leadership Articles

Clients want thought leadership articles because they signal industry authority and expertise.

This is valuable for forward-leaning companies like:

• AI
• Biotech
• Crypto

They want to show they're at the forefront of innovation.

5. Ghostwriting Website Content

Website copy is crucial because it's the first impression people have of a business.

It needs to clearly communicate:

• What the company does 
• Why someone should care

If you don't provide that, the reader will leave the website in seconds.

—
📌 The service I recommend getting started with?

LinkedIn Social Ghostwriting.

I put together a 30-day roadmap to help you land your first (or next) LinkedIn ghostwriting client—with 7 AI prompts to make it 10x easier.

Want access?

Comment "roadmap" and I'll DM it to you.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: You're not behind.
entries.postBody:
You're not behind.

You're still becoming.

I see people in their 20s panicking because they haven't "made it" yet.

People in their 30s thinking they missed their chance.

People in their 40s believing it's too late.

But here's the truth:

Life doesn't follow a timeline.

- Lost at 22.
- Broke at 28.
- Unsure at 31.
- Rising at 35.
- Entrepreneur at 38.
- Building a dream life at 44.

There's no deadline for success.

I was 38 when I started my business.

Burned out. Starting from scratch. No clue what I was doing.

By 44, I built a $10M+ business.

Not because I was ahead.

Because I trusted the process and kept moving.

Most people quit because they compare their chapter 3 to someone else's chapter 20.

But your story is yours.

Stop rushing.
Stop comparing.
Just keep building.

Greatness isn't rushed.

It's earned one day at a time.

This is exactly why I built The Creator MBA.

111 lessons showing you how to turn "starting over" into "taking over."

Everything I know about becoming an entrepreneur:

- Building an audience
- Choosing a profitable niche
- Creating your first offer
- Making it irresistable

It's 75% off for early Black Friday: https://buff.ly/JZMm8gU

(ending soon...)

Your timeline is perfect.
Now go build something on it.

entries.creator: Justin Welsh
entries.hook: Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.
entries.postBody:
Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.

They're waiting for:

The perfect idea.
The right economy.
More money in the bank.
Better market conditions.

But here's what I learned after building multiple companies:

There's never a perfect time to start anything.

Your idea will never feel ready.
The economy will always have problems.
You'll never have enough money saved.
The market will always be "uncertain."

The people who succeed aren't the ones who wait for perfect conditions.

They're the ones who start anyway.

They launch before they're ready.
They sell before the product is perfect.
They hire before they can afford it.

Because they know something most people don't:

You can't steer a parked car.

Movement creates momentum.
Action creates opportunity.
Starting creates solutions.

Every successful business owner I know has one thing in common:

They stopped planning and started building.

They figured it out along the way.
They adapted when things didn't work.
They kept moving when others stood still.

Your business will never be perfect.

But it can be profitable.

Stop waiting for everything to align.
Start building something that matters.

Want to see exactly how I built my first profitable business while everything was falling apart around me? 

I share the messy truth about starting when nothing feels right.

Read my complete story here: https://lnkd.in/eABypvdg

entries.creator: Lara Acosta
entries.hook: I spent the last 90 days testing every LinkedIn hack.
entries.postBody:
I spent the last 90 days testing every LinkedIn hack. 

Here's what actually works in 2026 (for any niche):

Everyone is talking about the "new" LinkedIn algorithm.
But I just see it as more of the same, but 10x more specific.

I've grown brands from 0-100,000 followers here.
Grown my own to 300,000+ in the same time.

Mastered every format here, and helped others too.

And there are 3 core elements you need to implement:

1. Hyper-specific content (3x a week)

• Solve one exact problem per post
• Use industry language your clients use
• Speak directly to one person

Jake Ward mastered this for "boring" SEO.
Built kleo.so to $60kmrr  as a result of exactly that 

Alex Vacca mastered this for GTM.
Scaled his business to $6M ARR too

Their content is hyper-specific but simple.

Clients want to work with them because they get it.
Users want to engage with them because they simplify it.

2. Proof-First Content

80% tactical step-by-step frameworks and screenshots.
20% stories on how you're doing it (use stories)

The 80% builds trust, the 20% engages them more. 

Educate and storytell. Always both. 

3. LinkedIn Video (let me explain...)

Video reach might be down right now.
But it's the fastest way to prove authority. 

LinkedIn video is ok.
Linkedin Lives? They're a goldmine.

We had a LinkedIn live with 200 people.
Made $10,000 from it. 

People buy from people they trust. Video accelerates this! 

I'd limit these to 2 a month, though, no pressure. 

I left a checklist for you in the image below for you to script your first Linkedin video too btw.

This is the no-bs blueprint for entrepreneurs. 

It's not about more volume, it's about more insights.

Dominating LinkedIn isn't just about virality right now.
It's about building a brand that earns trust.

Keep your focus on your 1 thing. 

The rest will follow.

PS: Who is your favorite "expert-led" creator right now? I want to follow more people! Tag them below! 

PPS: Jan 22nd, I'm walking you through the best way to master these 3 elements in 2026!

join us for free LIVE on the 22nd of January here: https://lnkd.in/eC5sm4cP

We're already at 4,000 sign ups! Come join us! 

See you thursday, love - Lara x
